steps.title: Set up ground plane geometry
steps.content:
Begin by drawing the horizontal ground plane. Mark the position of tower $A$ and draw a North line pointing "up" the page. Since the bearing of $B$ from $A$ is $090^\circ$ (East), draw a horizontal line $250$ m to the right to mark the base of tower $B$. Add a North line at $B$ parallel to the one at $A$.

This establishes the coordinate baseline for the 3D construction.

highlights.text: bearing of B from A is 090^\circ

steps.title: Plot drone ground position
steps.content:
To find the drone's location relative to the ground, draw the bearings from each tower base:

1. From $A$, draw a line on the ground plane at $042^\circ$ clockwise from North.
2. From $B$, draw a line on the ground plane at $330^\circ$ clockwise from North (equivalent to $30^\circ$ West of North).

Mark the intersection of these two ground lines as point $P$. This point represents the projection of the drone on the ground.

highlights.text: bearing of the drone from A is 042^\circ

steps.title: Construct vertical heights
steps.content:
Now add the vertical dimension to the 3D sketch:

1. Draw a vertical line up from $A$ labeled $40$ m.
2. Draw a vertical line up from $B$ labeled $70$ m.
3. Draw a vertical line up from the intersection point $P$ to represent the drone's altitude. Mark the top of this line as $D$ (the drone).

steps.title: Connect lines of sight
steps.content:
Finally, draw the lines of sight from the tops of the towers to the drone $D$:

1. Draw a straight line from the top of tower $A$ to $D$. Mark the angle of elevation $25^\circ$ between this line and the horizontal.
2. Draw a straight line from the top of tower $B$ to $D$. Mark the angle of elevation $21.3^\circ$ between this line and the horizontal.

highlights.text: angle of elevation of D is 25^\circ

steps.title: Verify diagram components
steps.content:
Review your diagram against the markscheme requirements. It must clearly show:
- The two towers with correct heights ($40$ m, $70$ m)
- Ground separation ($250$ m) and North lines
- Bearings ($042^\circ$, $330^\circ$) determining the ground position
- Non-coplanar lines of sight with elevation angles ($25^\circ$, $21.3^\circ$)

This complete 3D sketch visualizes the geometry needed for subsequent calculations.

highlights.text: Diagram shows both towers at appropriate bearings and the non-coplanar lines of sight

parts.content:
Let the point vertically below the drone be $P$ on the ground.
Find the horizontal distances $AP$ and $BP$.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify angles in triangle $ABP$: $\angle A = 90 - 42 = 48^\circ$ and $\angle B = 330 - 270 = 60^\circ$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Calculate $\angle P = 180 - 48 - 60 = 72^\circ$
- Use sine rule: $\frac{AP}{\sin 60^\circ} = \frac{250}{\sin 72^\circ}$ OR $\frac{BP}{\sin 48^\circ} = \frac{250}{\sin 72^\circ}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- $AP = 228$ m and $BP = 195$ m <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Analyze the horizontal geometry
steps.content:
We need to find the horizontal distances $AP$ and $BP$ in the triangle formed by the two towers ($A$, $B$) and the point directly below the drone ($P$).

First, let's determine the internal angles of the horizontal triangle $\triangle ABP$ using the given bearings.

From the question:
- The bearing of $B$ from $A$ is $090^\circ$ (due East).
- The bearing of the drone ($P$) from $A$ is $042^\circ$.

highlights.text: horizontal bearing of B from A is $090^\circ$
bearing of the drone from A is $042^\circ$

steps.title: Calculate angle at A
steps.content:
The internal angle at $A$, denoted as $\angle PAB$, is the difference between the bearing of $B$ and the bearing of $P$.

$$\angle PAB = 90^\circ - 42^\circ = 48^\circ$$

steps.title: Calculate angle at B
steps.content:
Now we find the angle at $B$. Since $B$ is due East of $A$, the bearing of $A$ from $B$ is $270^\circ$ (due West).

The bearing of the drone from $B$ is given as $330^\circ$.

The internal angle $\angle PBA$ is the difference between these two directions:
$$\angle PBA = 330^\circ - 270^\circ = 60^\circ$$

steps.title: Calculate the third angle
steps.content:
The sum of angles in a triangle is always $180^\circ$. We can find the remaining angle $\angle APB$:

$$\angle APB = 180^\circ - (48^\circ + 60^\circ)$$
$$\angle APB = 180^\circ - 108^\circ = 72^\circ$$

calculator.description: Find the third angle of the triangle

steps.title: Apply Sine Rule
steps.content:
We now have all three angles and one side ($AB = 250$ m). We can use the Sine Rule to find the unknown sides $AP$ and $BP$.

From the **Trigonometry** section of the data booklet:
$$\frac{a}{\sin A} = \frac{b}{\sin B} = \frac{c}{\sin C}$$

Applying this to our triangle $\triangle ABP$:
$$\frac{AP}{\sin(60^\circ)} = \frac{BP}{\sin(48^\circ)} = \frac{250}{\sin(72^\circ)}$$

steps.title: Calculate distance AP
steps.content:
Solve for $AP$:

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{AP}{\sin(60^\circ)} &= \frac{250}{\sin(72^\circ)} \\ AP &= \frac{250 \times \sin(60^\circ)}{\sin(72^\circ)} \\ AP &\approx 228 \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Ensure mode is Degree. Enter: 250 * sin(60) / sin(72)

ti84.instructions: Ensure mode is Degree. Enter: 250 * sin(60) / sin(72)

desmos.instructions: Make sure to use degrees. Enter: 250 * sin(60) / sin(72)

calculator.description: Calculate the length of side AP

steps.title: Calculate distance BP
steps.content:
Solve for $BP$:

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{BP}{\sin(48^\circ)} &= \frac{250}{\sin(72^\circ)} \\ BP &= \frac{250 \times \sin(48^\circ)}{\sin(72^\circ)} \\ BP &\approx 195 \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the length of side BP

steps.title: Determine angles from bearings
steps.content:
First, we find the internal angles of the triangle on the ground, $\triangle ABP$, using the given bearings.

For angle $A$:
- The bearing of $B$ from $A$ is $090^\circ$ (East).
- The bearing of $D$ (and thus $P$) from $A$ is $042^\circ$.
- Angle $A = 90^\circ - 42^\circ = 48^\circ$

For angle $B$:
- The bearing of $A$ from $B$ is $270^\circ$ (West, since $A$ to $B$ is East).
- The bearing of $D$ from $B$ is $330^\circ$.
- Angle $B = 330^\circ - 270^\circ = 60^\circ$

highlights.text: horizontal bearing of B from A is 090
bearing of the drone from A is 042

steps.title: Split into two right triangles
steps.content:
To solve without using the sine rule, we split the non-right triangle $\triangle ABP$ into two right-angled triangles.

Drop a perpendicular from point $P$ to the line segment $AB$. Let the intersection point be $Q$. This creates two right triangles: $\triangle AQP$ and $\triangle BQP$.

Let the perpendicular height $PQ = h$.
Let the distance $AQ = x$.
Since the total distance $AB = 250$ m, the remaining distance $QB = 250 - x$.

highlights.text: Two observation towers, A and B, stand on level ground 250 m apart

steps.title: Set up simultaneous equations
steps.content:
We can express the height $h$ in terms of $x$ for both triangles using the tangent ratio, $\tan \theta = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{adjacent}}$:

In $\triangle AQP$: 
$$\tan 48^\circ = \frac{h}{x} \implies h = x \tan 48^\circ$$

In $\triangle BQP$: 
$$\tan 60^\circ = \frac{h}{250 - x} \implies h = (250 - x) \tan 60^\circ$$

steps.title: Solve for x
steps.content:
Equate the two expressions for $h$ to solve for $x$:

$$\begin{aligned} x \tan 48^{\circ} &= (250 - x) \tan 60^{\circ} \\ x \tan 48^{\circ} &= 250 \tan 60^{\circ} - x \tan 60^{\circ} \\ x \tan 48^{\circ} + x \tan 60^{\circ} &= 250 \tan 60^{\circ} \\ x (\tan 48^{\circ} + \tan 60^{\circ}) &= 250 \tan 60^{\circ} \\ x &= \frac{250 \tan 60^{\circ}}{\tan 48^{\circ} + \tan 60^{\circ}} \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression to find x:

steps.title: Calculate distance AP
steps.content:
Using the calculated value $x \approx 152.33$ m, we find the hypotenuse $AP$ in $\triangle AQP$ using the cosine ratio, $\cos \theta = \frac{\text{adjacent}}{\text{hypotenuse}}$:

$$\begin{aligned} \cos 48^\circ &= \frac{x}{AP} \\ AP &= \frac{x}{\cos 48^\circ} \\ AP &= \frac{152.33}{\cos 48^\circ} \\ AP \approx 228 \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Divide the previous answer (x) by cos(48):

calculator.description: Calculate AP using the value of x

steps.title: Calculate distance BP
steps.content:
Similarly, find the hypotenuse $BP$ in $\triangle BQP$. The adjacent side is $QB = 250 - x$.

$$\begin{aligned} QB &= 250 - 152.33 = 97.67 \text{ m} \\ \cos 60^\circ &= \frac{QB}{BP} \\ BP &= \frac{QB}{\cos 60^\circ} \\ BP &= \frac{97.67}{0.5} \\ BP \approx 195 \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: First find QB, then divide by cos(60):

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
The horizontal distances are:

$$AP = 228 \text{ m}$$
$$BP = 195 \text{ m}$$

parts.content: Hence find the height of the drone above the ground and its straight-line 3-D distance from the top of tower $A$.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate height from $A$: $h = 40 + 227.6 \tan 25^\circ$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Height $h = 146$ m <marks>A1</marks>
- Calculate distance $AD$: $AD = \sqrt{AP^2 + (h-40)^2} = \sqrt{227.6^2 + (146.1-40)^2}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- $AD = 251$ m <marks>A1</marks>

<note>Allow follow through from Part 2. Accept values correct to 3 s.f.</note>

4 marks total

steps.title: Retrieve horizontal distance
steps.content:
From the previous part (Part 2), we found the horizontal distance from Tower A to the drone. Let's call this horizontal distance $AP$.

$$AP = 227.6 \text{ m}$$

steps.title: Set up height calculation
steps.content:
We need to find the total height of the drone, $h$. From the top of Tower A, the angle of elevation is $25^\circ$. We can view this as a right-angled triangle where:
- The adjacent side is the horizontal distance $AP = 227.6 \text{ m}$
- The opposite side is the height of the drone *above* Tower A, which is $(h - 40)$

Using the tangent ratio:

$$\tan(25^\circ) = \frac{h - 40}{227.6}$$

highlights.text: angle of elevation of D is 25^\circ

steps.title: Calculate drone height
steps.content:
Rearranging the equation to solve for $h$:

$$\begin{aligned} h - 40 &= 227.6 \times \tan(25^\circ) \\ h &= 40 + 227.6 \tan(25^\circ) \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: State the height
steps.content:
Calculating the value:

$$\begin{aligned} h &\approx 40 + 106.13 \\ h &\approx 146.13 \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to 3 significant figures, the height of the drone is **146 m**.

steps.title: Calculate slant distance AD
steps.content:
Now we need the straight-line distance from the top of Tower A to the drone. This is the hypotenuse of our right-angled triangle.

We can use Pythagoras' theorem with the horizontal distance ($227.6$) and the vertical height above A ($146.13 - 40 = 106.13$):

$$AD = \sqrt{227.6^2 + (146.13 - 40)^2}$$

steps.title: State the distance
steps.content:
Calculating the final value:

$$\begin{aligned} AD &= \sqrt{227.6^2 + 106.13^2} \\ AD &\approx \sqrt{51801.76 + 11263.58} \\ AD &\approx 251.13 \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

Alternatively, you could use the cosine ratio: $AD = \frac{227.6}{\cos(25^\circ)} \approx 251 \text{ m}$.

Rounding to 3 significant figures, the distance is **251 m**.

steps.title: Analyze horizontal angles
steps.content:
First, we model the situation on the horizontal ground plane. Let $P$ be the point on the ground directly below the drone. We form a triangle with the bases of towers $A$ and $B$ and point $P$.

Using the given bearings:
- The bearing of $B$ from $A$ is $090^\circ$ (East). The bearing of the drone from $A$ is $042^\circ$. The angle at $A$ is:
  $$90^\circ - 42^\circ = 48^\circ$$

- The bearing of $A$ from $B$ is $270^\circ$ (West). The bearing of the drone from $B$ is $330^\circ$. The angle at $B$ is:
  $$330^\circ - 270^\circ = 60^\circ$$

- The third angle at $P$ is:
  $$180^\circ - 48^\circ - 60^\circ = 72^\circ$$

highlights.text: horizontal bearing of B from A is $090^\circ$
bearing of the drone from A is $042^\circ$

steps.title: Calculate horizontal distance from B
steps.content:
We need the horizontal distance from tower $B$ to the drone (distance $BP$) to use Tower B's geometry. We apply the **Sine Rule** to the horizontal triangle.

$$\frac{BP}{\sin 48^\circ} = \frac{250}{\sin 72^\circ}$$

Solving for $BP$:

steps.title: State horizontal distance BP
steps.content:
$$BP \approx 195.35 \text{ m}$$

We keep this precise value for the next calculation.

steps.title: Calculate drone height
steps.content:
Now we use the vertical right-angled triangle at Tower $B$ to find the drone's height $h$. Tower $B$ is $70$ m high, and the angle of elevation is $21.3^\circ$.

The height of the drone above the top of Tower $B$ is $BP \tan(21.3^\circ)$.

The total height $h$ above the ground is:
$$h = 70 + 195.35 \times \tan(21.3^\circ)$$

steps.title: State the height
steps.content:
$$h \approx 146.16 \text{ m}$$

To 3 significant figures, the height is **146 m**.

steps.title: Calculate distance from Tower A
steps.content:
Finally, we calculate the straight-line 3-D distance $AD$ from the top of Tower $A$ using the height we just found.

Tower $A$ is $40$ m high. The vertical distance from the top of $A$ to the drone is:
$$h - 40 = 146.16 - 40 = 106.16 \text{ m}$$

Using the angle of elevation from $A$ ($25^\circ$) in the vertical right-angled triangle formed by the line of sight:
$$\sin(25^\circ) = \frac{\text{Opposite}}{\text{Hypotenuse}} = \frac{106.16}{AD}$$

Rearranging for $AD$:
$$AD = \frac{106.16}{\sin(25^\circ)}$$

highlights.text: from $A$, the angle of elevation of $D$ is $25^\circ$

steps.title: State the final distance
steps.content:
$$AD \approx 251.2 \text{ m}$$

To 3 significant figures, the distance is **251 m**.

**Final Answer:**
Height: **146 m**
Distance AD: **251 m**

parts.content:
The drone now circles tower $A$ at constant height $146.1$ m, describing a horizontal circle of radius 200 m.
A camera on $A$ can rotate through a maximum of $160^\circ$ before needing to reset.
Determine the length of visible path of the drone from $A$ before the camera must reset, giving your answer to the nearest metre.
parts.markscheme:
- Convert $160^\circ$ to radians: $\theta = 160\pi/180 = 2.7925$ rad <marks>M1</marks>
- Arc length $s = r\theta = 200(2.7925)$ <marks>M1</marks>
- $s = 559$ m <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Identify the arc length formula
steps.content:
To find the length of the visible path, we use the arc length formula from the **Geometry and trigonometry** section of the data booklet. 

It is important to note that this formula requires the angle $\theta$ to be in **radians**.

steps.title: Convert angle to radians
steps.content:
The question gives the angle of rotation in degrees. We must convert this to radians by multiplying by $\frac{\pi}{180^{\circ}}$.

$$\begin{aligned} \theta &= 160^{\circ} \times \frac{\pi}{180^{\circ}} \\ &= \frac{160\pi}{180} \\ &= \frac{8\pi}{9} \approx 2.7925 \text{ rad} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate the arc length
steps.content:
Now substitute the radius $r = 200$ m and the angle $\theta = \frac{8\pi}{9}$ rad into the formula $l = r\theta$.

$$\begin{aligned} l &= 200 \times \frac{8\pi}{9} \\ &= \frac{1600\pi}{9} \\ &\approx 558.505... \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Multiply the radius by the angle in radians:

ti84.instructions: Multiply the radius by the angle in radians:

desmos.instructions: Multiply the radius by the angle in radians:

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Round the result to the nearest metre as required by the question.

$$l \approx 559 \text{ m}$$

steps.title: Identify radius and angle
steps.content: First, we identify the key dimensions of the drone's path from the question. The drone moves in a horizontal circle around Tower A with a specific radius, and the camera tracks it over a specific angle.

steps.title: Calculate total circumference
steps.content:
To find the visible path length, we first consider the circumference of the full circle. The formula for the circumference of a circle is $C = 2\pi r$. 

Substituting our radius $r = 200$:

$$C = 2\pi(200) = 400\pi$$

steps.title: Apply proportion of circle
steps.content:
The camera does not see the full circle, only a portion corresponding to $160^{\circ}$. We calculate the length of this arc by multiplying the total circumference by the fraction of the circle covered ($360^{\circ}$ is a full circle).

$$\text{Arc Length} = 2\pi r \times \frac{\theta}{360}$$

$$\text{Arc Length} = 400\pi \times \frac{160}{360}$$

steps.title: Calculate final length
steps.content:
Now we compute the value:

$$\text{Arc Length} = 400\pi \times \frac{4}{9} = \frac{1600\pi}{9} \approx 558.505\dots \text{ m}$$

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The question asks for the answer to the **nearest metre**. 

$$558.505\dots \approx 559 \text{ m}$$

parts.content:
During this circular motion, the drone begins a climb such that its height increases proportionally to the square of the horizontal angle turned:
\[
h(\theta) = 146.1 + 2.5\theta^2 \quad (0 \le \theta \le 2.7925),
\]
where $\theta$ is in radians.
Find, correct to three significant figures,
1. the total 3-D distance flown by the drone over this visible path, and
2. the greatest angle of elevation of the drone from the top of tower A.
parts.markscheme:
- Horizontal differential arc element: $ds_h = r\,d\theta = 200\,d\theta$
- Vertical rise rate: $\frac{dh}{d\theta} = 5\theta$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Differential element of 3-D path: $ds = \sqrt{200^2 + (5\theta)^2}\,d\theta$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Integrate $s = \int_0^{2.7925}\sqrt{40000 + 25\theta^2}\,d\theta$ using calculator <marks>M1</marks>
- $s = 559$ m <marks>A1</marks>
- Greatest elevation occurs at final height $h=146.1+2.5(2.7925)^2=165.6$ m
- $\tan\alpha=\frac{165.6-40}{200}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- $\alpha \approx 32.1^\circ$ <marks>A1</marks>

6 marks total

steps.title: Analyze the 3D motion
steps.content:
To find the total 3-D distance, we must integrate the infinitesimal path length $ds$ over the given angle interval. The drone's motion has two components:
1.  **Horizontal circular motion**: Following the markscheme and solution context, the drone moves on a circle with radius $r = 200$ m. The horizontal arc length element is $ds_h = r \, d\theta$.
2.  **Vertical climb**: The height $h$ changes with $\theta$, contributing a vertical component $dh$.

steps.title: Define horizontal arc rate
steps.content:
For a circular path of radius $r=200$ m, the rate of change of horizontal distance with respect to the angle $\theta$ is constant.

$$ds_h = 200 \, d\theta$$

steps.title: Find vertical rate of change
steps.content:
Differentiate the given height function $h(\theta)$ with respect to $\theta$ to find the vertical rate: $\frac{dh}{d\theta} = 5\theta$.

$$\begin{aligned} h(\theta) &= 146.1 + 2.5\theta^2 \\ \frac{dh}{d\theta} &= 2 \times 2.5\theta \\ &= 5\theta \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Formulate 3D arc length integral
steps.content:
Using the Pythagorean relationship for the differential element $ds = \sqrt{ds_h^2 + dh^2}$, we construct the integral for the total distance $s$.

$$\begin{aligned} s &= \int_{0}^{2.7925} \sqrt{(200)^2 + (5\theta)^2} \, d\theta \\ &= \int_{0}^{2.7925} \sqrt{40000 + 25\theta^2} \, d\theta \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate total distance
steps.content:
Evaluate the definite integral using your calculator.

$$s \approx 559 \text{ m}$$

gdc.instructions: In Run-Matrix mode, press OPTN > CALC > ∫dx. Enter √(40000 + 25x^2) with limits 0 and 2.7925.

ti84.instructions: Press MATH > 9 (fnInt). Enter the function sqrt(40000 + 25x^2), variable x, lower limit 0, upper limit 2.7925.

desmos.instructions: Type 'int' to create an integral, enter bounds 0 and 2.7925, and integrand sqrt(40000 + 25x^2) dx.

calculator.description: Evaluate the definite integral for arc length

steps.title: Identify greatest elevation
steps.content:
The angle of elevation $\alpha$ from Tower A depends on the drone's height $h(\theta)$ and the horizontal distance $r=200$. Since $h(\theta)$ increases with $\theta$, the maximum elevation occurs at the maximum angle $\theta = 2.7925$.

Calculate the final height:
$$h(2.7925) = 146.1 + 2.5(2.7925)^2$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the value of h at theta = 2.7925

ti84.instructions: Calculate the value of h at theta = 2.7925

desmos.instructions: Calculate the value of h at theta = 2.7925

steps.title: Calculate elevation angle
steps.content:
Using the calculated maximum height of $165.6$ m and the tower height of $40$ m, we find the angle of elevation $\alpha$ using $\tan \alpha = \frac{165.6 - 40}{200}$:

$$\begin{aligned} \tan \alpha &= \frac{\text{Opposite}}{\text{Adjacent}} = \frac{165.6 - 40}{200} \\ \tan \alpha &= \frac{125.6}{200} \\ \alpha &= \arctan\left(\frac{125.6}{200}\right) \end{aligned}$$

This gives $\alpha \approx 32.1^{\circ}$.

gdc.instructions: Calculate the inverse tangent. Ensure mode is set to DEGREES.

ti84.instructions: Calculate the inverse tangent. Ensure mode is set to DEGREES.

steps.title: State final answers
steps.content:
The total distance is **559 m**.

The greatest angle of elevation is $\alpha \approx 32.1^{\circ}$.

steps.title: Identify the model parameters
steps.content:
To solve this problem using the **Unrolled Surface Model**, we map the cylindrical motion onto a 2D plane. The drone moves in a circle while climbing.

First, we need the radius of the circular motion. Based on the provided markscheme solution, we use the radius $r = 200$ m.

*Note: Calculating the radius directly from the bearings and coordinates in the question text yields approximately 227.6 m. The value 200 m is likely established in a previous part of the question or specific to the markscheme version provided. We will proceed with $r = 200$ m to explain the official solution.*

steps.title: Horizontal arc length element
steps.content:
In the unrolled model, the horizontal circular motion is treated as a linear distance $x$. The arc length $x$ for an angle $\theta$ (in radians) is given by $x = r\theta$.

Using $r = 200$:
$$x = 200\theta$$

The rate of change of horizontal distance with respect to $\theta$ is:
$$\frac{dx}{d\theta} = 200$$

steps.title: Vertical rate of change
steps.content:
Next, we examine the vertical motion. The height $h$ is given as a function of $\theta$:

$$h(\theta) = 146.1 + 2.5\theta^2$$

We differentiate this with respect to $\theta$ to find the vertical rate of change: $\frac{dh}{d\theta} = 5\theta$.

$$\frac{dh}{d\theta} = \frac{d}{d\theta}(146.1 + 2.5\theta^2) = 5\theta$$

steps.title: Construct the 3-D integral
steps.content:
The total 3-D distance flown is the arc length of the path. Using the parametric arc length formula $s = \int_a^b \sqrt{(\frac{dx}{d\theta})^2 + (\frac{dh}{d\theta})^2} \, d\theta$:

$$s = \int_0^{2.7925} \sqrt{(200)^2 + (5\theta)^2} \, d\theta$$

Simplifying the expression under the square root:

$$s = \int_0^{2.7925} \sqrt{40000 + 25\theta^2} \, d\theta$$

steps.title: Calculate total distance
steps.content: Use a graphing calculator to evaluate the definite integral.

gdc.instructions: In Run-Matrix mode, select [OPTN] > [CALC] > [∫dx]. Enter √(40000+25x²), lower bound 0, upper bound 2.7925.

ti84.instructions: Press [MATH] > 9:fnInt(. Enter the integrand sqrt(40000 + 25X^2), variable X, lower limit 0, upper limit 2.7925.

desmos.instructions: Type 'int' to create an integral. Set bounds from 0 to 2.7925. Enter sqrt(40000 + 25x^2) dx.

calculator.description: Evaluate the definite integral for distance

steps.title: Find maximum height
steps.content:
For the second part, we need the greatest angle of elevation. Since the horizontal distance $r=200$ is constant, the angle of elevation is greatest when the height $h$ is greatest.

The height increases with $\theta$, so the maximum height occurs at the maximum angle $\theta = 2.7925$:

$$h_{max} = 146.1 + 2.5(2.7925)^2$$

$$h_{max} \approx 146.1 + 19.495 = 165.6 \text{ m}$$

highlights.text: Greatest elevation occurs at final height $h=146.1+2.5(2.7925)^2=165.6$ m

steps.title: Calculate angle of elevation
steps.content:
The angle of elevation $\alpha$ from the top of Tower A is measured relative to the horizontal plane at Tower A's height (40 m). 

$$\tan \alpha = \frac{\text{vertical difference}}{\text{horizontal distance}}$$

$$\tan \alpha = \frac{165.6 - 40}{200} = \frac{125.6}{200}$$

$$\alpha = \arctan\left(\frac{125.6}{200}\right)$$

Which evaluates to $\alpha \approx 32.1^{\circ}$.

gdc.instructions: Ensure Angle unit is Degrees. Calculate arctan.

ti84.instructions: Make sure mode is set to DEGREE. Press [2nd] [TAN] (tan⁻¹) and enter the fraction.

desmos.instructions: Calculate arctan and convert to degrees if necessary.

steps.title: State final answers
steps.content:
1. The total 3-D distance flown is **559 m** (3 s.f.).
2. The greatest angle of elevation is $\alpha \approx 32.1^{\circ}$ (3 s.f.).

questions.specification:
Two cranes, $C_1$ and $C_2$, are $150 \text{ m}$ apart on a construction site.
The bearing of $C_2$ from $C_1$ is $120^\circ$.
A suspended load $L$ is vertically above $L_0$.
The bearing of $L_0$ from $C_1$ is $170^\circ$.
At a certain instant, the angle of elevation of the load from $C_1$ is $25^\circ$ and from $C_2$ is $28^\circ$.

labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Arc length of a circular path from a change in bearing/angle with given radius (part e).

parts.content: Draw a fully-labelled 3-D diagram showing $C_1, C_2, L_0, L$ and all known angles.
parts.markscheme:
- Diagram drawn with ground triangle $C_1C_2L_0$ and vertical line $LL_0$. <marks>M1</marks>
- Correct labelling of side $C_1C_2=150$ and bearings/angles (angle at $C_1 = 50^\circ$). <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Define the horizontal base
steps.content:
To begin the **3D Geometric Construction**, we first establish the horizontal plane representing the ground. Draw a triangle with vertices $C_1$, $C_2$, and $L_0$, where $L_0$ is the point on the ground directly below the load $L$.

Label the known distance between the cranes:
$$C_1C_2 = 150 \text{ m}$$

highlights.text: Two cranes, $C_1$ and $C_2$, are $150 \text{ m}$ apart

steps.title: Calculate the ground angle
steps.content:
Next, we determine the internal angle of the triangle at $C_1$. This angle is the difference between the bearing of $L_0$ and the bearing of $C_2$. 

$$\text{Angle at } C_1 = 170^\circ - 120^\circ = 50^\circ$$

Label the angle $\angle C_2C_1L_0$ as $50^\circ$ on your diagram.

steps.title: Construct the vertical height
steps.content:
Now, add the vertical dimension to your construction. Draw a vertical line segment $LL_0$ rising from point $L_0$ perpendicular to the ground triangle. 

Point $L$ represents the load, and $L_0$ is its projection on the ground.

steps.title: Label angles of elevation
steps.content:
Finally, draw lines of sight from each crane ($C_1$ and $C_2$) to the load $L$. These form vertical right-angled triangles ($ \triangle L L_0 C_1$ and $\triangle L L_0 C_2$).

Mark the angles of elevation:
- Angle $\angle LC_1L_0 = 25^\circ$
- Angle $\angle LC_2L_0 = 28^\circ$

highlights.text: angle of elevation of the load from $C_1$ is $25^\circ$ and from $C_2$ is $28^\circ$

steps.title: Final diagram summary
steps.content:
Your complete 3D diagram should show:
1.  **Base Triangle**: Horizontal triangle $C_1C_2L_0$ with side $C_1C_2 = 150$ and $\angle C_2C_1L_0 = 50^\circ$.
2.  **Vertical Height**: Line $LL_0$ standing perpendicular to the base at $L_0$.
3.  **Elevation Triangles**: Two vertical right triangles connecting the cranes to the load, labeled with $25^\circ$ at $C_1$ and $28^\circ$ at $C_2$.

parts.content:
Let the horizontal distances $C_1 L_0 = x$ m and $C_2 L_0 = y$ m.
Show that
$$
x\tan25^\circ = y\tan28^\circ
$$
parts.markscheme:
- State the expression for height $h$ from $C_1$: $h = x\tan25^\circ$. <marks>A1</marks>
- State the expression for height $h$ from $C_2$: $h = y\tan28^\circ$. <marks>M1</marks>
- Equate the expressions to show the result: $x\tan25^\circ = y\tan28^\circ$. <marks>AG</marks>

steps.title: Identify vertical right-angled triangles
steps.content:
To connect the horizontal distances $x$ and $y$ with the vertical height, we need to look at the vertical cross-sections. 

Let $h$ be the vertical height of the load $L$ above point $L_0$ (the length $L_0L$). This height $h$ is common to two separate vertical right-angled triangles formed with each crane:
1. Triangle $C_1 L_0 L$ containing the angle of elevation $25^\circ$
2. Triangle $C_2 L_0 L$ containing the angle of elevation $28^\circ$

highlights.text: angle of elevation of the load from $C_1$ is $25^\circ$

steps.title: Express height from Crane 1
steps.content:
In the right-angled triangle for Crane 1 ($C_1$), the horizontal distance is $x$ and the vertical height is $h$. 

Using the definition of tangent ($$\tan \theta = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{adjacent}}$$), we can write an expression for $h$:

$$\begin{aligned} \tan 25^\circ &= \frac{h}{x} \\ h &= x \tan 25^\circ \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Express height from Crane 2
steps.content:
Similarly, in the right-angled triangle for Crane 2 ($C_2$), the horizontal distance is $y$ and the vertical height is the same $h$.

Using the tangent ratio again:

$$\begin{aligned} \tan 28^\circ &= \frac{h}{y} \\ h &= y \tan 28^\circ \end{aligned}$$

highlights.text: horizontal distances $C_1 L_0 = x$ m and $C_2 L_0 = y$ m

steps.title: Equate the two expressions
steps.content:
Since both expressions represent the same vertical height $h$, we can equate them to find the relationship between $x$ and $y$:

$$x \tan 25^\circ = y \tan 28^\circ$$

parts.content: Using the cosine rule in triangle $C_1C_2L_0$, write an equation for $y^2$ in terms of $x$ and known constants.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the angle at $C_1$: $170^\circ - 120^\circ = 50^\circ$. <marks>A1</marks>
- Apply the Cosine Rule: $y^2 = 150^2 + x^2 - 2(150)(x)\cos(50^\circ)$. <marks>M1 A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify triangle components
steps.content:
First, let's identify the sides and angle in triangle $C_1C_2L_0$ that we need for the cosine rule:

*   Side $C_1C_2 = 150\text{ m}$ (distance between cranes)
*   Side $C_1L_0 = x$ (distance from crane 1 to the load's ground position)
*   Side $C_2L_0 = y$ (distance from crane 2 to the load's ground position)
*   The included angle is at vertex $C_1$.

steps.title: Calculate the included angle
steps.content:
The angle at $C_1$ is the difference between the two bearings from $C_1$. 

We subtract the bearing of $C_2$ from the bearing of $L_0$:

$$\text{Angle } C_2C_1L_0 = 170^\circ - 120^\circ = 50^\circ$$

steps.title: Select the Cosine Rule
steps.content: Since we know two sides ($150$ and $x$) and the included angle ($50^\circ$), we use the Cosine Rule from the data booklet to find the third side $y$.

steps.title: Substitute values into formula
steps.content:
Substitute $a = 150$, $b = x$, and $C = 50^\circ$ into the formula to express $y^2$:

$$y^2 = 150^2 + x^2 - 2(150)(x)\cos(50^\circ)$$

parts.content:
Combine your results from parts (b) and (c) to find the height $h$ of the load.
Give your answer to the nearest tenth of a metre.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute $y = x \frac{\tan25^\circ}{\tan28^\circ} \approx 0.877x$ into the Cosine Rule equation. <marks>M1</marks>
- Attempt to solve the quadratic equation for $x$ (e.g., $0.231x^2 - 192.8x + 22500 = 0$). <marks>M1</marks>
- Obtain $x \approx 140.2$ (rejecting $x \approx 695$ as unrealistic/inconsistent). <marks>A1</marks>
- Calculate $h$: $h=140.2\tan25^\circ \approx 65.4 \text{ m}$. <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Recall relationships from previous parts
steps.content:
From parts (b) and (c), we have defined the horizontal distances from the cranes to the load as $x = C_1L_0$ and $y = C_2L_0$. We established the relationship between these distances using the height $h$:

$$h = x \tan 25^\circ = y \tan 28^\circ \implies y = x \frac{\tan 25^\circ}{\tan 28^\circ}$$

Also, the angle between the two cranes at $C_1$ is the difference in their bearings:
$$\text{Angle at } C_1 = 170^\circ - 120^\circ = 50^\circ$$

steps.title: Apply the Cosine Rule
steps.content:
We apply the Cosine Rule to the triangle formed by the cranes ($C_1, C_2$) and the load's ground position ($L_0$). The side opposite the known angle ($50^\circ$) is $y$.

Using the formula
$$c^{2}=a^{2}+b^{2}-2ab\cos C$$
where $c=y$, $a=x$, $b=150$, and $C=50^\circ$:

$$y^2 = x^2 + 150^2 - 2(x)(150)\cos 50^\circ$$

steps.title: Substitute and form a quadratic
steps.content:
Substitute the expression $y = x \frac{\tan 25^\circ}{\tan 28^\circ}$ into the Cosine Rule equation:

$$\left(x \frac{\tan 25^\circ}{\tan 28^\circ}\right)^2 = x^2 + 22500 - 300x \cos 50^\circ$$

Calculate the coefficients:
- $\frac{\tan 25^\circ}{\tan 28^\circ} \approx 0.877$
- $300 \cos 50^\circ \approx 192.8$

Rearranging into the form $ax^2 + bx + c = 0$:
$$x^2(0.877^2) = x^2 + 22500 - 192.8x$$
$$0.769x^2 - x^2 + 192.8x - 22500 = 0$$
$$-0.231x^2 + 192.8x - 22500 = 0$$
$$0.231x^2 - 192.8x + 22500 = 0$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the ratio and squared ratio

ti84.instructions: Calculate the ratio and squared ratio

desmos.instructions: Calculate the ratio and squared ratio

calculator.description: Calculate coefficients for the quadratic equation

steps.title: Solve for x
steps.content:
Use the quadratic formula or a polynomial solver to find $x$. The equation is:
$$0.231x^2 - 192.8x + 22500 = 0$$

Using the quadratic formula:
$$x = \frac{192.8 \pm \sqrt{(-192.8)^2 - 4(0.231)(22500)}}{2(0.231)}$$

This gives two possible values:
$$x \approx 140.2 \text{ m} \quad \text{or} \quad x \approx 695.0 \text{ m}$$

ti84.instructions: Use PolyRootFinder or graph y = 0.231x^2 - 192.8x + 22500 and find zeros

desmos.instructions: Graph the quadratic and find x-intercepts

calculator.description: Solve the quadratic equation for x

steps.title: Determine the correct height
steps.content:
We reject the larger value $x \approx 695$ as it leads to an unrealistic site dimension inconsistent with the setup. Using $x \approx 140.2$, we calculate the height $h$:

$$h = x \tan 25^\circ$$
$$h = 140.2 \times \tan 25^\circ$$
$$h \approx 65.4 \text{ m}$$

steps.title: Define variables and find the angle
steps.content:
First, let's define the horizontal distances from each crane to the load's position on the ground, $L_0$:
- Let $x$ be the distance $C_1L_0$
- Let $y$ be the distance $C_2L_0$

We need the angle between the two cranes at $C_1$. This is the difference in their bearings:
$$\theta = 170^\circ - 120^\circ = 50^\circ$$

steps.title: Formulate equations from previous parts
steps.content:
From the angles of elevation, we can express the height $h$ in terms of $x$ and $y$. Since $h = x\tan25^\circ$ and $h = y\tan28^\circ$, we can find $y$ in terms of $x$:

$$y = x \frac{\tan25^\circ}{\tan28^\circ}$$

Next, we use the **Cosine Rule** for the horizontal triangle formed by the cranes and the load:

$$y^2 = x^2 + 150^2 - 2(x)(150)\cos50^\circ$$

steps.title: Combine into a single equation
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the expression for $y$ into the Cosine Rule equation to create a single equation with only one variable, $x$:

$$\left(x \frac{\tan25^\circ}{\tan28^\circ}\right)^2 = x^2 + 150^2 - 300x\cos50^\circ$$

This is a complicated quadratic equation, so we will use the GDC Numerical Solver to find $x$.

steps.title: Solve for x using GDC
steps.content:
Enter the equation into your graphing calculator's numerical solver. 

The solver may find two positive roots: $x \approx 140.2$ and $x \approx 695$. The larger value represents an unrealistically large distance and height for a standard construction site, so we reject it and use $x \approx 140.2$.

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to Equation/Solver mode
2. Enter: (x*tan(25)/tan(28))^2 = x^2 + 150^2 - 300*x*cos(50)
3. Set a reasonable initial guess (e.g., 100)
4. Solve

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [MATH] > [Solver] (or 'Numeric Solver')
2. Enter eqn: E1: (X*tan(25)/tan(28))^2 and E2: X^2+150^2-300*X*cos(50)
3. Press [OK] or [GRAPH]
4. Guess: 100, then press [ALPHA] [ENTER] (SOLVE)

desmos.instructions:
Type the equation: (x * tan(25 deg) / tan(28 deg))^2 = x^2 + 150^2 - 300x * cos(50 deg)
Click on the intersection point near x=140.

calculator.description: Solve the combined equation for x

steps.title: Calculate the height h
steps.content:
Finally, use the value of $x$ to find the height $h$. We know from the angle of elevation that:

$$h = x \tan25^\circ$$

Substitute $x \approx 140.237$:

$$h = 140.237 \times \tan25^\circ \approx 65.39$$

Rounding to the nearest tenth of a metre:

$$h \approx 65.4 \text{ m}$$

steps.title: Analyze the ground geometry
steps.content:
First, let's visualize the triangle formed on the ground by the two crane bases $C_1$, $C_2$ and the point $L_0$ directly below the load.

We know:
- The distance between cranes is $C_1C_2 = 150 \text{ m}$.
- The bearing of $C_2$ from $C_1$ is $120^\circ$.
- The bearing of $L_0$ from $C_1$ is $170^\circ$.

The angle $\angle C_2 C_1 L_0$ is the difference between these bearings:
$$\theta = 170^\circ - 120^\circ = 50^\circ$$

steps.title: Express distances in terms of h
steps.content:
We need to find the horizontal distances from each crane to the load point $L_0$ in terms of the height $h$. Using the right-angled triangles formed by the height $h$ and the horizontal distances:

For crane $C_1$ (angle of elevation $25^\circ$):
$$\tan 25^\circ = \frac{h}{C_1L_0} \implies C_1L_0 = \frac{h}{\tan 25^\circ}$$

For crane $C_2$ (angle of elevation $28^\circ$):
$$\tan 28^\circ = \frac{h}{C_2L_0} \implies C_2L_0 = \frac{h}{\tan 28^\circ}$$

highlights.text: angle of elevation of the load from $C_1$ is $25^\circ$

steps.title: Apply the Cosine Rule
steps.content:
Now, we apply the Cosine Rule to the ground triangle $\triangle C_1 C_2 L_0$ to link these distances together.

Using the formula $c^2 = a^2 + b^2 - 2ab \cos C$ from the data booklet:
$$(C_2L_0)^2 = (C_1L_0)^2 + (C_1C_2)^2 - 2(C_1L_0)(C_1C_2)\cos(50^\circ)$$

Substituting our expressions in terms of $h$:
$$\left(\frac{h}{\tan 28^\circ}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{h}{\tan 25^\circ}\right)^2 + 150^2 - 2\left(\frac{h}{\tan 25^\circ}\right)(150)\cos 50^\circ$$

steps.title: Form the quadratic equation
steps.content:
Let's rearrange this equation into a standard quadratic form $Ah^2 + Bh + C = 0$ to solve for $h$ directly.

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{h^2}{\tan^2 28^\circ} &= \frac{h^2}{\tan^2 25^\circ} + 22500 - \frac{300h \cos 50^\circ}{\tan 25^\circ} \\ 0 &= h^2\left(\frac{1}{\tan^2 25^\circ} - \frac{1}{\tan^2 28^\circ}\right) - h\left(\frac{300 \cos 50^\circ}{\tan 25^\circ}\right) + 22500 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate coefficients
steps.content:
Use your calculator to find the numerical values for the coefficients.

Coefficient of $h^2$:
$$A = \frac{1}{\tan^2 25^\circ} - \frac{1}{\tan^2 28^\circ} \approx 1.062$$

Coefficient of $h$:
$$B = -\frac{300 \cos 50^\circ}{\tan 25^\circ} \approx -413.5$$

Constant term:
$$C = 22500$$

So our quadratic equation is approx:
$$1.062h^2 - 413.5h + 22500 = 0$$

gdc.instructions:
1. Calculate A: (1/tan(25))^2 - (1/tan(28))^2
2. Calculate B: -300 * cos(50) / tan(25)

ti84.instructions:
1. Calculate A: (1/tan(25))^2 - (1/tan(28))^2
2. Calculate B: -300 * cos(50) / tan(25)

desmos.instructions: Type (1/tan(25 deg))^2 - (1/tan(28 deg))^2 for A and -300*cos(50 deg)/tan(25 deg) for B

steps.title: Solve for h
steps.content:
Use the quadratic formula or your calculator's polynomial solver to find $h$.

$$h = \frac{-(-413.5) \pm \sqrt{(-413.5)^2 - 4(1.062)(22500)}}{2(1.062)}$$

The calculator gives two positive roots:
$$h_1 \approx 65.4 \text{ m}$$
$$h_2 \approx 324.1 \text{ m}$$

Given the context, a load height of $324 \text{ m}$ is physically unrealistic compared to the crane spacing. We reject the larger value.

gdc.instructions:
Go to Equation mode -> Polynomial -> Degree 2.
Enter coefficients 1.062, -413.5, 22500.
Press SOLVE.

ti84.instructions:
Press APPS, select PLYSMLT2, then ROOT FINDER.
Enter Order 2.
Enter coefficients: A=1.062, B=-413.5, C=22500.
Press SOLVE.

steps.title: Final answer
steps.content:
The realistic height of the load is approximately $65.4 \text{ m}$.

$$h \approx 65.4 \text{ m}$$

parts.content:
The load's projection $L_0$ describes a circular path of radius $140 \text{ m}$.
Calculate, in metres, the arc length when the bearing from $C_1$ to $L_0$ changes from $170^\circ$ to $200^\circ$.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the central angle: $\theta = 200^\circ - 170^\circ = 30^\circ = \frac{\pi}{6} \text{ rad}$. <marks>M1 A1</marks>
- Calculate the arc length: $s=r\theta=140 \times (\frac{\pi}{6}) \approx 73.3 \text{ m}$. <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the given information
steps.content: To find the arc length, we first identify the radius of the circular path and the change in angle. The problem states the path has a radius of $140 \text{ m}$ and the bearing from $C_1$ changes from $170^\circ$ to $200^\circ$.

highlights.text: bearing from $C_1$ to $L_0$ changes from $170^\circ$ to $200^\circ$

steps.title: Calculate the angle in degrees
steps.content:
We calculate the central angle $\theta$ by finding the difference between the final and initial bearings.

$$\theta_{\text{deg}} = 200^\circ - 170^\circ = 30^\circ$$

steps.title: Convert degrees to radians
steps.content:
The arc length formula in the IB data booklet requires the angle to be in **radians**. We convert $30^\circ$ to radians by multiplying by $\frac{\pi}{180}$.

$$\theta_{\text{rad}} = 30 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = \frac{30\pi}{180} = \frac{\pi}{6} \text{ rad}$$

steps.title: Apply the arc length formula
steps.content:
Now we use the formula for the length of an arc from the data booklet, substituting $r = 140$ and $\theta = \frac{\pi}{6}$.

$$l = \theta r$$

$$l = \frac{\pi}{6} \times 140$$

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Calculate the numerical value and round to 3 significant figures.

$$l \approx 73.3038... \text{ m}$$

$$l \approx 73.3 \text{ m}$$

steps.title: Find change in angle
steps.content:
First, we determine the angle through which the load's projection $L_0$ moves. The question states the bearing changes from an initial value to a final value. We find the difference between these bearings.

$$\theta = 200^\circ - 170^\circ = 30^\circ$$

highlights.text: bearing from $C_1$ to $L_0$ changes from $170^\circ$ to $200^\circ$

steps.title: Set up arc length formula
steps.content:
Using the **Degree Proportion Approach**, we calculate the arc length $s$ as a fraction of the circle's full circumference ($C = 2\pi r$). The fraction is determined by the central angle $\theta$ divided by the full circle $360^\circ$.

$$s = \frac{\theta}{360^\circ} \times 2\pi r$$

steps.title: Substitute values and calculate
steps.content:
Now we substitute $\theta = 30^\circ$ and $r = 140$ into the equation to solve for the arc length.

$$\begin{aligned} s &= \frac{30}{360} \times 2\pi(140) \\ &= \frac{1}{12} \times 280\pi \\ &= \frac{70\pi}{3} \\ &\approx 73.3038\dots \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content: Rounding the result to 3 significant figures, the arc length is $73.3 \text{ m}$.

parts.content: The tension in the two supporting cables is proportional to their length, $T = k \cdot \text{Length}$. Find the ratio $\dfrac{T_1}{T_2}$ at this instant, where $T_1$ and $T_2$ are the tensions in the cables from $C_1$ and $C_2$ respectively.
parts.markscheme:
- State the relationship: $\dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = \dfrac{|C_1L|}{|C_2L|}$. <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate the lengths using $x \approx 140.2, y \approx 123.0, h \approx 65.4$: $|C_1L| = \sqrt{x^2+h^2} \approx 154.7$, $|C_2L| = \sqrt{y^2+h^2} \approx 139.3$. <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate the ratio: $\dfrac{154.7}{139.3} \approx 1.11$. <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify tension-length relationship
steps.content:
The question states that tension is proportional to length ($$T = k \cdot \text{Length}$$). Since $k$ is a constant, the ratio of the tensions is equal to the ratio of the cable lengths.

We can write this relationship as:
$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = \frac{k \cdot |C_1L|}{k \cdot |C_2L|} = \frac{|C_1L|}{|C_2L|}$$

highlights.text: The tension in the two supporting cables is proportional to their length

steps.title: Recall calculated distances
steps.content:
From previous parts of the problem, we have determined the horizontal distances from each crane to the point $L_0$ directly below the load, as well as the vertical height $h$.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Horizontal distance } |C_1L_0| &= x \approx 140.2 \text{ m} \\ \text{Horizontal distance } |C_2L_0| &= y \approx 123.0 \text{ m} \\ \text{Vertical height } |LL_0| &= h \approx 65.4 \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate length of cable 1
steps.content:
The cable forms the hypotenuse of a right-angled triangle with the horizontal distance $x$ and height $h$. We use the Pythagorean theorem to calculate the length $|C_1L|$.

$$|C_1L| = \sqrt{x^2 + h^2} = \sqrt{140.2^2 + 65.4^2}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the slant length of the first cable

steps.title: State length of cable 1
steps.content:
Performing the calculation:

$$|C_1L| \approx 154.7 \text{ m}$$

steps.title: Calculate length of cable 2
steps.content:
Similarly, we find the length of the second cable $|C_2L|$ using the horizontal distance $y$ and height $h$.

$$|C_2L| = \sqrt{y^2 + h^2} = \sqrt{123.0^2 + 65.4^2}$$

$$|C_2L| \approx 139.3 \text{ m}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the slant length of the second cable

steps.title: Calculate the tension ratio
steps.content:
Finally, we divide the length of cable 1 by the length of cable 2 to find the ratio of the tensions.

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = \frac{154.7}{139.3}$$

calculator.description: Divide the two lengths to find the ratio

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The ratio of the tensions is approximately:

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} \approx 1.11$$

steps.title: State the tension ratio
steps.content:
Since the problem states that tension is proportional to length ($T = k \times \text{Length}$), the ratio of the tensions $T_1$ and $T_2$ is equal to the ratio of the lengths of the cables, $L_1$ and $L_2$.

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = \frac{L_1}{L_2}$$

highlights.text: The tension in the two supporting cables is proportional to their length

steps.title: Express lengths using height
steps.content:
Let $h$ be the common vertical height of the load $L$ above the ground ($L_0$). We can use the sine ratio from trigonometry, defined as $\sin \theta = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{hypotenuse}}$, to express the length of each cable (hypotenuse) in terms of $h$ (opposite).

For the cable from $C_1$ with an angle of elevation of $25^\circ$:
$$\sin 25^\circ = \frac{h}{L_1} \implies L_1 = \frac{h}{\sin 25^\circ}$$

For the cable from $C_2$ with an angle of elevation of $28^\circ$:
$$\sin 28^\circ = \frac{h}{L_2} \implies L_2 = \frac{h}{\sin 28^\circ}$$

highlights.text: angle of elevation of the load from $C_1$ is $25^\circ$ and from $C_2$ is $28^\circ$

steps.title: Simplify the ratio
steps.content:
Now substitute these expressions into our ratio equation. Using this method allows the unknown height $h$ to cancel out completely, leaving a direct calculation based on the angles.

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = \frac{\frac{h}{\sin 25^\circ}}{\frac{h}{\sin 28^\circ}} = \frac{h}{\sin 25^\circ} \times \frac{\sin 28^\circ}{h}$$

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = \frac{\sin 28^\circ}{\sin 25^\circ}$$

gdc.instructions:
1. In Run-Matrix mode, ensure Angle unit is set to Degree (Shift > Menu > Angle)
2. Type sin(28) ÷ sin(25)
3. Press EXE

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [MODE] and ensure Degree mode is selected
2. Press [2nd] [QUIT]
3. Type sin(28) / sin(25)
4. Press [ENTER]

desmos.instructions:
1. Type sin(28 deg) / sin(25 deg)
2. Note: Make sure to specify degrees or change settings to degrees

calculator.description: Calculate the ratio of the sines

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Evaluating the expression:

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} \approx \frac{0.4695}{0.4226} \approx 1.11$$

The ratio of the tensions is $1.11$.

steps.title: Establish the tension ratio
steps.content:
The question states that tension is proportional to length ($T = k \times \text{Length}$). This means the ratio of the tensions is equal to the ratio of the cable lengths:

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = \frac{\text{Length}_1}{\text{Length}_2}$$

highlights.text: The tension in the two supporting cables is proportional to their length

steps.title: Recall horizontal distances
steps.content:
From previous parts of the question (as indicated in the markscheme values), we have the horizontal distances from the cranes to the point directly below the load:

*   Horizontal distance from $C_1$: $$x \approx 140.2 \text{ m}$$ 
*   Horizontal distance from $C_2$: $$y \approx 123.0 \text{ m}$$

steps.title: Formula for cable length
steps.content:
Using the **horizontal distance** method, we can find the hypotenuse (cable length) using the cosine ratio in a right-angled triangle:

$$\cos \theta = \frac{\text{adjacent}}{\text{hypotenuse}} = \frac{\text{horizontal distance}}{\text{cable length}}$$

Rearranging for length:

$$\text{Length} = \frac{\text{horizontal distance}}{\cos \theta}$$

steps.title: Calculate length of Cable 1
steps.content:
For Crane 1, the angle of elevation is $25^\circ$ and the horizontal distance is $140.2 \text{ m}$.

$$|C_1L| = \frac{140.2}{\cos 25^\circ} \approx 154.7 \text{ m}$$

gdc.instructions: Ensure mode is set to Degree. Type 140.2 / cos(25).

ti84.instructions: Ensure mode is set to Degree. Type 140.2 divided by cos(25).

desmos.instructions: Type 140.2 / cos(25). Ensure settings are in Degrees.

highlights.text: elevation of the load from $C_1$ is $25^\circ$

steps.title: Calculate length of Cable 2
steps.content:
For Crane 2, the angle of elevation is $28^\circ$ and the horizontal distance is $123.0 \text{ m}$.

$$|C_2L| = \frac{123.0}{\cos 28^\circ} \approx 139.3 \text{ m}$$

steps.title: Calculate the ratio
steps.content:
Finally, we calculate the ratio $\frac{T_1}{T_2}$ using the calculated lengths:

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = \frac{154.7}{139.3} \approx 1.11$$

gdc.instructions: Divide the previous result for L1 by L2.

ti84.instructions: Divide the previous result for L1 by L2.

questions.specification:
A light flare $F$ is positioned vertically above a point $F_0$ on the ground.
Two observers, $O_A$ and $O_B$, are 60 m apart.
The bearing of $O_B$ from $O_A$ is $150^\circ$. The bearing of the flare $F_0$ from $O_A$ is $200^\circ$.
At a certain instant, the angle of elevation of the flare from $O_A$ is $30^\circ$ and from $O_B$ is $37^\circ$.
