questions.specification:
A radio mast $P$ is held upright by two guy wires attached to stakes $A$ and $B$ on level ground.
The distance $AB$ is 120 m. The mast is vertically above point $P_0$, and in the horizontal triangle $ABP_0$ the angle $\angle AP_0B = 55^\circ$.

The angles of elevation of the top of the mast are $27^\circ$ from $A$ and $36^\circ$ from $B$.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Geometry & Trigonometry > SL 3.3—Angles of elevation and depression, bearings > Question Type 3: Applying values of elevation and depression
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 3: Applying values of elevation and depression
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses angles of elevation from two ground points to relate mast height to horizontal distances (tan relationships) and solve a real-world 3D setup.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Geometry & Trigonometry > SL 3.2—2d and 3d trig, sine rule, cosine rule, area > Question Type 4: Using cosine rule to find the 3rd side
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 4: Using cosine rule to find the 3rd side
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Requires applying the cosine rule in triangle ABP0 to form an equation relating x and y (given AB and included angle).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Geometry & Trigonometry > SL 3.2—2d and 3d trig, sine rule, cosine rule, area > Question Type 1: Using SOH CAH TOA to find one side length given an angle
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 1: Using SOH CAH TOA to find one side length given an angle
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Computes unknown lengths (e.g., mast height and wire lengths) from right-triangle trig using tan/cos once angles and adjacent sides are known.

parts.content: Draw a fully-labelled 3-D diagram showing $A, B, P_0, P$ and all given angles.
parts.markscheme:
- Draw triangle $ABP_0$ with $AB=120\text{ m}$, $\angle AP_0B=55^\circ$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Draw vertical line $PP_0$ and indicate angles of elevation $27^\circ$ and $36^\circ$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Direct 3D Sketching approach
steps.content: To visualize this 3D problem, we will build the diagram in two stages: first, the horizontal ground plane in perspective, and second, the vertical mast rising from it. This helps separate the right-angled vertical triangles from the non-right-angled ground triangle.

steps.title: Draw the ground triangle
steps.content:
Draw a triangle $ABP_0$ on a "flat" plane to represent the level ground. Because we are viewing it in perspective, it should look slightly squashed.

Label the vertices $A$, $B$, and $P_0$.

steps.title: Draw the vertical mast
steps.content:
Draw a vertical line rising straight up from $P_0$ to a point $P$. This represents the radio mast. 

Since the mast is vertical, the line $PP_0$ is perpendicular to the horizontal plane.

steps.title: Connect the guy wires
steps.content: Draw straight lines connecting the top of the mast $P$ to the stakes $A$ and $B$. These lines represent the hypotenuses of the vertical right-angled triangles.

highlights.text: two guy wires attached to stakes $A$ and $B$

steps.title: Label ground measurements
steps.content:
Add the given measurements to the horizontal triangle:
- Label the side $AB$ as $120\text{ m}$.
- Label the angle at the base of the mast, $\angle AP_0B$, as $55^\circ$.

steps.title: Label angles of elevation
steps.content:
The angle of elevation is measured from the horizontal ground up to the wire.

- Mark the angle at $A$ (inside triangle $PAP_0$) as $27^\circ$.
- Mark the angle at $B$ (inside triangle $PBP_0$) as $36^\circ$.

steps.title: Final Diagram Review
steps.content:
Your final 3-D sketch should clearly show:
- A horizontal triangle $ABP_0$ with $\angle P_0 = 55^\circ$ and $AB=120$.
- A vertical mast $PP_0$.
- Two vertical right-angled triangles $PAP_0$ and $PBP_0$ sharing the side $PP_0$, with base angles $27^\circ$ and $36^\circ$ respectively.

parts.content:
Let $AP_0=x$ m and $BP_0=y$ m.
Show that
$$
x\tan27^\circ = y\tan36^\circ.
$$
parts.markscheme:
- From right triangles: $h = x\tan27^\circ$ and $h = y\tan36^\circ$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Equating the expressions for $h$: $x\tan27^\circ = y\tan36^\circ$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify vertical triangles
steps.content:
To solve this using the **Equating Vertical Height** method, we first identify the two vertical right-angled triangles that share the mast height as a common side.

Let $h$ be the height of the mast $PP_0$.

*   **Triangle 1:** $\triangle APP_0$ with base $x$ and angle of elevation $27^\circ$.
*   **Triangle 2:** $\triangle BPP_0$ with base $y$ and angle of elevation $36^\circ$.

This involves concepts from **Topic 3: Geometry & Trigonometry** (specifically right-angled trigonometry).

highlights.text: angles of elevation of the top of the mast are $27^\circ$ from $A$ and $36^\circ$ from $B$

steps.title: Express height for triangle A
steps.content:
In the right-angled triangle $APP_0$, we use the tangent ratio ($$\tan \theta = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{adjacent}}$$) to express the height $h$ in terms of $x$.

$$\tan 27^\circ = \frac{h}{x}$$

Rearranging for $h$:

$$h = x \tan 27^\circ$$

steps.title: Express height for triangle B
steps.content:
Similarly, in the right-angled triangle $BPP_0$, we express the same height $h$ in terms of $y$ using the tangent ratio.

$$\tan 36^\circ = \frac{h}{y}$$

Rearranging for $h$:

$$h = y \tan 36^\circ$$

steps.title: Equate the heights
steps.content:
Since both expressions represent the same height $h$ of the mast, we can equate them to form an equation connecting $x$ and $y$:

$$x \tan 27^\circ = y \tan 36^\circ$$

This matches the required expression.

steps.title: Analyze triangle AP₀P
steps.content:
First, let's look at the vertical right-angled triangle $AP_0P$. We know the angle of elevation at $A$ is $27^\circ$, and the mast is vertical, so $\angle PP_0A = 90^\circ$. 

We can calculate the third angle at the top of the mast:
$$\angle APP_0 = 180^\circ - 90^\circ - 27^\circ = 63^\circ$$

highlights.text: angles of elevation of the top of the mast are $27^\circ$ from $A$

steps.title: Apply Sine Rule to AP₀P
steps.content:
Now we apply the sine rule to relate the height $h$ (side $PP_0$) and the horizontal distance $x$ (side $AP_0$):

$$\frac{h}{\sin 27^\circ} = \frac{x}{\sin 63^\circ}$$

Using the identity $\sin(90^\circ - \theta) = \cos \theta$, we know that $\sin 63^\circ = \cos 27^\circ$. Substituting this back gives:

$$\frac{h}{\sin 27^\circ} = \frac{x}{\cos 27^\circ} \Rightarrow h = x\frac{\sin 27^\circ}{\cos 27^\circ} = x\tan 27^\circ$$

steps.title: Analyze triangle BP₀P
steps.content:
Similarly, for the vertical triangle $BP_0P$, the angle of elevation is $36^\circ$. The angle at the top is:
$$\angle BPP_0 = 180^\circ - 90^\circ - 36^\circ = 54^\circ$$

Applying the sine rule again:
$$\frac{h}{\sin 36^\circ} = \frac{y}{\sin 54^\circ}$$

Using the identity $\sin 54^\circ = \cos 36^\circ$, we simplify:
$$h = y\frac{\sin 36^\circ}{\cos 36^\circ} = y\tan 36^\circ$$

steps.title: Equate expressions for h
steps.content:
Since both expressions represent the same height $h$ of the mast, we can equate them:

$$x\tan 27^\circ = y\tan 36^\circ$$

This confirms the required relationship.

parts.content: Using the cosine rule in triangle $ABP_0$, write an equation relating $x$ and $y$.
parts.markscheme:
- State the cosine rule equation: $120^2 = x^2 + y^2 - 2xy\cos55^\circ$ <marks>M1A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify triangle components
steps.content:
We need to relate the side lengths $x$ and $y$ in the horizontal triangle $ABP_0$. 

From the question, we identify:
*   The included angle $\angle AP_0B = 55^\circ$
*   The side opposite this angle is $AB = 120$ m
*   The sides adjacent to the angle are $x$ and $y$

steps.title: Choose the cosine rule
steps.content:
Since we have two sides ($x$ and $y$), the included angle ($55^\circ$), and the opposite side ($120$), the cosine rule is the appropriate method. This is covered in **Topic 3: Geometry and Trigonometry**.

From the data booklet:
$$c^2 = a^2 + b^2 - 2ab \cos C$$

steps.title: Substitute into formula
steps.content:
We substitute our specific values into the formula:
*   $c = 120$ (the side opposite the angle)
*   $a = x$ and $b = y$ (the adjacent sides)
*   $C = 55^\circ$ (the angle)

$$120^2 = x^2 + y^2 - 2(x)(y)\cos 55^\circ$$

gdc.instructions: Type 120, press the x² button, then EXE.

ti84.instructions: Type 120, press the x² button, then ENTER.

steps.title: State the final equation
steps.content:
The question asks for an equation relating $x$ and $y$. The unsimplified form matches the method marks.

$$120^2 = x^2 + y^2 - 2xy\cos 55^\circ$$

You may also write $14400$ instead of $120^2$, but leaving it squared is sufficient.

parts.content: Find the height $h$ of the mast, correct to the nearest metre.
parts.markscheme:
- From Part 2: $y = \dfrac{x\tan27^\circ}{\tan36^\circ}$
- Substitute into equation from Part 3 and solve for $x$:
  $120^2 = x^2 + \Bigl(\dfrac{x\tan27^\circ}{\tan36^\circ}\Bigr)^2 - 2x\Bigl(\dfrac{x\tan27^\circ}{\tan36^\circ}\Bigr)\cos55^\circ$ <marks>M1</marks>
- This gives $x \approx 144.7\text{ m}$, $y \approx 101.5\text{ m}$
- Calculate height $h = x\tan27^\circ \approx 144.7\tan27^\circ \approx 73.8\text{ m}$
- Conclude final answer: $h \approx 74\text{ m}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Express ground distances
steps.content:
First, let's define the variables for the ground distances. Let $x = AP_0$ and $y = BP_0$. Using simple right-angled trigonometry on the two vertical triangles formed by the mast and the stakes:

$$h = x \tan 27^\circ \implies x = \frac{h}{\tan 27^\circ}$$
$$h = y \tan 36^\circ \implies y = \frac{h}{\tan 36^\circ}$$

Since both expressions equal $h$, we can relate $y$ directly to $x$:
$$x \tan 27^\circ = y \tan 36^\circ \implies y = x \frac{\tan 27^\circ}{\tan 36^\circ}$$

steps.title: Apply Cosine Rule
steps.content:
Now consider the horizontal triangle on the ground, $\triangle ABP_0$. We know side $c = 120$, angle $C = 55^\circ$, and sides $x$ and $y$. We apply the Cosine Rule from the data booklet:

$$c^2 = a^2 + b^2 - 2ab \cos C$$

Substituting our values gives:
$$120^2 = x^2 + y^2 - 2xy \cos 55^\circ$$

Substitute the expression for $y$ in terms of $x$ into this equation:
$$120^2 = x^2 + \left( x \frac{\tan 27^\circ}{\tan 36^\circ} \right)^2 - 2x \left( x \frac{\tan 27^\circ}{\tan 36^\circ} \right) \cos 55^\circ$$

steps.title: Solve for x
steps.content:
This equation allows us to solve for the ground distance $x$. Factor out $x^2$:

$$14400 = x^2 \left[ 1 + \left(\frac{\tan 27^\circ}{\tan 36^\circ}\right)^2 - 2\left(\frac{\tan 27^\circ}{\tan 36^\circ}\right)\cos 55^\circ \right]$$

Using a calculator to evaluate the bracketed term:
$$\text{Let } k = \frac{\tan 27^\circ}{\tan 36^\circ} \approx 0.7013$$
$$14400 = x^2 [ 1 + (0.7013)^2 - 2(0.7013)\cos 55^\circ ]$$
$$14400 \approx x^2 [ 0.6873 ]$$

Solving for $x$:
$$x = \sqrt{\frac{14400}{0.6873}} \approx 144.74 \text{ m}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression to solve for x directly:

desmos.instructions: Type the equation to find x:

steps.title: Calculate height h
steps.content:
Finally, substitute the value of $x$ back into the height equation $h = x \tan 27^\circ$:

$$h \approx 144.74 \times \tan 27^\circ$$
$$h \approx 73.75 \text{ m}$$

Rounding to the nearest metre as required:
$$h \approx 74 \text{ m}$$

gdc.instructions: Multiply the previous answer by tan(27):

ti84.instructions: Multiply the previous answer by tan(27):

desmos.instructions: Calculate h using the value of x:

steps.title: Define horizontal distances
steps.content:
To find the height $h$ directly, we first need to express the horizontal distances from the base of the mast, $P_0$, to the stakes $A$ and $B$ in terms of $h$.

Using basic right-angled trigonometry (SOH CAH TOA) on the vertical triangles $\triangle APP_0$ and $\triangle BPP_0$:

$$\tan(\text{angle}) = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{adjacent}} = \frac{h}{\text{distance}}$$

Therefore:
$$\begin{aligned} AP_0 &= \frac{h}{\tan 27^\circ} \\ BP_0 &= \frac{h}{\tan 36^\circ} \end{aligned}$$

highlights.text: angles of elevation of the top of the mast are $27^\circ$ from $A$ and $36^\circ$ from $B$

steps.title: Select Cosine Rule
steps.content:
Now consider the horizontal triangle on the ground, $\triangle ABP_0$. We know the side $AB = 120\text{ m}$ and the angle between the other two sides, $\angle AP_0B = 55^\circ$.

Since we have two sides (expressed in terms of $h$) and the included angle, we use the **Cosine Rule** from the **Trigonometry** section of the data booklet.

steps.title: Substitute into Cosine Rule
steps.content:
Substitute $c = 120$, $a = AP_0$, $b = BP_0$, and $C = 55^\circ$ into the formula:

$$120^2 = \left(\frac{h}{\tan 27^\circ}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{h}{\tan 36^\circ}\right)^2 - 2\left(\frac{h}{\tan 27^\circ}\right)\left(\frac{h}{\tan 36^\circ}\right)\cos 55^\circ$$

steps.title: Isolate the height variable
steps.content:
We can factor out $h^2$ from the right-hand side to solve for $h$. This creates a direct equation for the height:

$$14400 = h^2 \left[ \frac{1}{(\tan 27^\circ)^2} + \frac{1}{(\tan 36^\circ)^2} - \frac{2 \cos 55^\circ}{\tan 27^\circ \tan 36^\circ} \right]$$

$$h = \sqrt{\frac{14400}{\frac{1}{(\tan 27^\circ)^2} + \frac{1}{(\tan 36^\circ)^2} - \frac{2 \cos 55^\circ}{\tan 27^\circ \tan 36^\circ}}}$$

steps.title: Calculate the value
steps.content:
Enter the expression into your calculator carefully. Be sure to use parentheses around the denominators.

$$h \approx \sqrt{\frac{14400}{3.851 + 1.894 - 3.099}}$$

$$h \approx \sqrt{\frac{14400}{2.646}}$$

$$h \approx 73.75...$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the equation or expression directly

ti84.instructions: Enter the full expression to avoid rounding errors

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
The question asks for the height correct to the nearest metre.

$$h \approx 74\text{ m}$$

steps.title: Express sides in terms of h
steps.content:
We need to find the mast height $h$. Let's express the horizontal distances $AP_0$ and $BP_0$ in terms of $h$ using the right-angled triangles formed by the mast and the ground.

Using the definition of tangent ($$\tan \theta = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{adjacent}}$$):

$$\begin{aligned} \tan 27^\circ &= \frac{h}{AP_0} \implies AP_0 = \frac{h}{\tan 27^\circ} \\ \tan 36^\circ &= \frac{h}{BP_0} \implies BP_0 = \frac{h}{\tan 36^\circ} \end{aligned}$$

highlights.text: angles of elevation of the top of the mast are $27^\circ$ from $A$ and $36^\circ$ from $B$

steps.title: Set up the Cosine Rule equation
steps.content:
In the horizontal triangle $ABP_0$, we know the side $AB = 120$ m and the included angle $\angle AP_0B = 55^\circ$. We can apply the Cosine Rule using the expressions for $AP_0$ and $BP_0$ we just found.

Using the formula $$c^2 = a^2 + b^2 - 2ab \cos C$$ where $c = AB$, $a = AP_0$, and $b = BP_0$:

$$120^2 = \left(\frac{h}{\tan 27^\circ}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{h}{\tan 36^\circ}\right)^2 - 2\left(\frac{h}{\tan 27^\circ}\right)\left(\frac{h}{\tan 36^\circ}\right)\cos 55^\circ$$

steps.title: Solve for h using a numerical solver
steps.content: Instead of rearranging this complex equation algebraically, use the numerical solver on your graphing calculator to find $h$ directly.

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to Equation mode > Solver (F3).
2. Enter the equation: 120^2 = (x/tan(27))^2 + (x/tan(36))^2 - 2*(x/tan(27))*(x/tan(36))*cos(55)
3. Set Lower/Upper bounds if needed (h > 0).
4. Press SOLVE.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [MATH] > [Solver] (or Numeric Solver).
2. Enter Equation 1 (E1): 120^2
3. Enter Equation 2 (E2): (X/tan(27))^2 + (X/tan(36))^2 - 2*(X/tan(27))*(X/tan(36))*cos(55)
4. Press [OK] or [Graph].
5. Place cursor on X= line, guess 50, and press [ALPHA] [ENTER] (SOLVE).

desmos.instructions:
Type the equation directly into a line to see the vertical solution line, or define functions f(h) and g(h) to find the intersection.
The x-coordinate of the intersection is the height.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The calculator gives a value of $x \approx 73.75...$ (which is our height $h$).

Rounding to the nearest metre as requested:
$$h \approx 74 \text{ m}$$

parts.content: If $T_A = k|AP|$ and $T_B = k|BP|$ are the tensions in the wires where $k$ is a constant, find the value of the ratio $\dfrac{T_A}{T_B}$.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate hypotenuse lengths:
  $AP = \sqrt{x^2 + h^2} \approx \sqrt{144.7^2 + 73.8^2} \approx 162.4\text{ m}$
  $BP = \sqrt{y^2 + h^2} \approx \sqrt{101.5^2 + 73.8^2} \approx 125.5\text{ m}$ <marks>M1M1</marks>
- Calculate the ratio: $\dfrac{T_A}{T_B} = \dfrac{162.4}{125.5} \approx 1.29$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Set up the ratio equation
steps.content:
The question asks for the ratio $\frac{T_A}{T_B}$. We are given expressions for the tension in terms of the wire lengths $AP$ and $BP$, where $k$ is a constant.

$$\frac{T_A}{T_B} = \frac{k|AP|}{k|BP|} = \frac{AP}{BP}$$

Since $k$ cancels out, we simply need to find the ratio of the lengths of the two wires.

steps.title: Retrieve calculated dimensions
steps.content:
From the working in previous parts of the question, we have found the height of the mast ($h$) and the horizontal distances from the base ($P_0$) to the stakes ($x$ for $AP_0$ and $y$ for $BP_0$).

Using the values consistent with the markscheme:
$$\begin{aligned} \text{Height } h &\approx 73.8 \text{ m} \\ \text{Distance } x &\approx 144.7 \text{ m} \\ \text{Distance } y &\approx 101.5 \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate length of wire AP
steps.content:
We can view the mast, the ground, and wire $A$ as a right-angled triangle. Using the Pythagorean theorem ($hyp^2 = adj^2 + opp^2$), we calculate the length of the hypotenuse $AP$.

$$\begin{aligned} AP &= \sqrt{x^2 + h^2} \\ AP &= \sqrt{144.7^2 + 73.8^2} \\ AP &\approx 162.4 \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the length of wire AP

steps.title: Calculate length of wire BP
steps.content:
Similarly, we use the Pythagorean theorem for wire $B$ using horizontal distance $y$ and height $h$.

$$\begin{aligned} BP &= \sqrt{y^2 + h^2} \\ BP &= \sqrt{101.5^2 + 73.8^2} \\ BP &\approx 125.5 \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the length of wire BP

steps.title: Calculate the final ratio
steps.content:
Finally, substitute these calculated lengths back into our ratio equation.

$$\frac{T_A}{T_B} = \frac{162.4}{125.5} \approx 1.29$$

steps.title: Analyze the tension ratio
steps.content:
The question asks for the ratio of the tensions $\dfrac{T_A}{T_B}$. We are given that the tension is proportional to the length of the wire with a constant $k$.

$$\begin{aligned} \dfrac{T_A}{T_B} &= \dfrac{k|AP|}{k|BP|} \\ \dfrac{T_A}{T_B} &= \dfrac{|AP|}{|BP|} \end{aligned}$$

Since $k$ cancels out, we just need to find the ratio of the wire lengths $|AP|$ and $|BP|$.

steps.title: Express lengths using sine
steps.content:
Let $h$ be the height of the mast $|PP_0|$. In the right-angled triangles formed by the mast and the wires, the mast height is the **opposite** side to the angle of elevation, and the wire length is the **hypotenuse**.

Using the fundamental trigonometric definition $\sin \theta = \frac{\text{Opp}}{\text{Hyp}}$:

For wire A (angle $27^\circ$):
$$\sin 27^\circ = \frac{h}{|AP|} \implies |AP| = \frac{h}{\sin 27^\circ}$$

For wire B (angle $36^\circ$):
$$\sin 36^\circ = \frac{h}{|BP|} \implies |BP| = \frac{h}{\sin 36^\circ}$$

highlights.text: angles of elevation of the top of the mast are 27^\circ from A and 36^\circ from B

steps.title: Simplify the ratio
steps.content:
Now we substitute these expressions back into our ratio equation. Notice that the height $h$ appears in both terms and will cancel out.

$$\begin{aligned} \dfrac{T_A}{T_B} &= \dfrac{\frac{h}{\sin 27^\circ}}{\frac{h}{\sin 36^\circ}} \\ &= \frac{h}{\sin 27^\circ} \times \frac{\sin 36^\circ}{h} \\ &= \frac{\sin 36^\circ}{\sin 27^\circ} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate the final value
steps.content:
We compute the value using the calculator.

$$\dfrac{T_A}{T_B} = \frac{\sin 36^\circ}{\sin 27^\circ} \approx 1.2947...$$

Rounding to 3 significant figures, the ratio is **1.29**.

gdc.instructions: In the Run-Matrix menu, ensure Angle is set to Deg. Type the expression.

ti84.instructions: Ensure you are in Degree mode. Calculate sin(36)/sin(27).

calculator.description: Calculate the ratio of the sines

questions.specification:
A signal beacon \(P\) is suspended by two cables from points \(A\) and \(B\) on level ground.  
The points \(A\) and \(B\) are 100 m apart, and \(P\) is directly above its ground projection \(P_0\).  
In the horizontal triangle \(ABP_0\), \(\angle AP_0B = 50^\circ\).

At a certain instant, the angle of elevation of the beacon from \(A\) is \(25^\circ\) and from \(B\) is \(40^\circ\).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Geometry & Trigonometry > SL 3.3—Angles of elevation and depression, bearings > Question Type 3: Applying values of elevation and depression
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 3: Applying values of elevation and depression
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses angles of elevation from two ground points to relate horizontal distances to the beacon’s height (parts a–d) and to form tangent relationships.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Geometry & Trigonometry > SL 3.2—2d and 3d trig, sine rule, cosine rule, area > Question Type 4: Using cosine rule to find the 3rd side
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 4: Using cosine rule to find the 3rd side
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Applies the cosine rule in triangle ABP0 with included angle 50° to relate x, y, and AB (part c), then uses it with the tangent relation to solve (part d).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Geometry & Trigonometry > SL 3.4—Circle, radians, arcs, sectors > Question Type 2: Calculating the arc length given a radius and specific angle
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 2: Calculating the arc length given a radius and specific angle
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Finds arc length on a circle given radius and change in bearing/central angle, requiring radians (part e).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Geometry & Trigonometry > SL 3.3—Angles of elevation and depression, bearings > Question Type 1: Finding angles using bearings
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 1: Finding angles using bearings
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Involves bearings (020° to 080°) to determine the angular change corresponding to the arc (part e).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Geometry & Trigonometry > SL 3.2—2d and 3d trig, sine rule, cosine rule, area > Question Type 1: Using SOH CAH TOA to find one side length given an angle
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 1: Using SOH CAH TOA to find one side length given an angle
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Computes slanted cable lengths using right-triangle trig (SOHCAHTOA) once height and horizontal distances are known, to form the tension ratio (part f).

parts.content: Draw a fully-labelled 3-D diagram showing \(A,B,P_0,P\) and all given angles.
parts.markscheme:
- Triangle \(ABP_0\) drawn with \(AB=100\text{ m}\) and \(\angle AP_0B=50^\circ\). <marks>M1</marks>  
- Vertical line \(PP_0\) and angles of elevation \(25^\circ\) at \(A\), \(40^\circ\) at \(B\) indicated. <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Visualize the setup
steps.content:
To build a 3D geometric sketch, we first identify the two planes involved:
1.  The **horizontal ground plane** containing points $A$, $B$, and $P_0$.
2.  The **vertical dimension** where the beacon $P$ hangs directly above $P_0$.

We will draw the ground triangle in perspective (slightly flattened) to give the illusion of depth.

highlights.text: points A and B on level ground
P is directly above its ground projection P_0

steps.title: Draw the ground triangle
steps.content:
Draw a triangle $ABP_0$ to represent the floor. Since we are drawing in 3D perspective, it helps to draw this triangle somewhat "flat".

Label the distance between $A$ and $B$ as $100\text{ m}$.

Indicate the horizontal angle $\angle AP_0B$ as $50^\circ$. This is the angle on the ground at point $P_0$.

highlights.text: A and B are 100 m apart

steps.title: Add the vertical beacon
steps.content:
Draw a vertical line upwards from $P_0$ to a point $P$. This represents the height of the beacon.

Since $P$ is directly above $P_0$, the line $PP_0$ is perpendicular to the ground plane. You can mark a right angle symbol at $P_0$ (where $PP_0$ meets lines $AP_0$ or $BP_0$) to emphasize this, though it is not strictly required if the vertical nature is clear.

steps.title: Draw elevation angles
steps.content:
Connect the top point $P$ to points $A$ and $B$ to form the cables (or lines of sight).

The **angle of elevation** is the angle between the horizontal ground line and the line of sight looking up.

*   At point $A$, label the angle between $AP_0$ and $AP$ as $25^\circ$.
*   At point $B$, label the angle between $BP_0$ and $BP$ as $40^\circ$.

highlights.text: angle of elevation of the beacon from A is 25^\circ

steps.title: Final Diagram Check
steps.content:
Your final diagram should include:
*   **Vertices:** $A$, $B$, $P_0$, and $P$.
*   **Lines:** Triangle $ABP_0$ on the bottom, vertical line $PP_0$, and hypotenuses $PA$ and $PB$.
*   **Labels:** 
    *   $100$ on side $AB$
    *   $50^\circ$ at angle $P_0$ (horizontal)
    *   $25^\circ$ at angle $PAP_0$ (vertical)
    *   $40^\circ$ at angle $PBP_0$ (vertical)

This fully labelled sketch earns the marks for showing the correct geometric relationships.

parts.content:
Let \(AP_0 = x\) m and \(BP_0 = y\) m.  
Show that  
\[
x\tan25^\circ = y\tan40^\circ.
\]
parts.markscheme:
- From right triangles: \(h = x\tan25^\circ\) and \(h = y\tan40^\circ\). <marks>M1</marks>  
- Therefore \(x\tan25^\circ = y\tan40^\circ\). <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Visualize the vertical triangles
steps.content:
To solve this problem, we need to visualize the 3D situation. The beacon $P$ is suspended above point $P_0$. This creates two vertical right-angled triangles:

1. Triangle $AP_0P$: containing the angle of elevation from $A$
2. Triangle $BP_0P$: containing the angle of elevation from $B$

Let $h$ be the vertical height of the beacon, which is the length of the side $PP_0$. This height $h$ is common to both triangles.

highlights.text: P is directly above its ground projection P_0

steps.title: Find height using point A
steps.content:
First, let's look at the triangle involving point $A$. We have a right-angled triangle with base $x$, height $h$, and an angle of $25^\circ$. 

Using the tangent ratio $$\tan\theta = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{adjacent}}$$:

$$ \tan 25^\circ = \frac{h}{x} $$

Rearranging to express $h$ in terms of $x$:

$$ h = x \tan 25^\circ $$

highlights.text: angle of elevation of the beacon from A is 25^\circ

steps.title: Find height using point B
steps.content:
Next, we apply the same logic to the triangle involving point $B$. This is a right-angled triangle with base $y$, height $h$, and an angle of $40^\circ$.

Using the tangent ratio again:

$$ \tan 40^\circ = \frac{h}{y} $$

Rearranging to express $h$ in terms of $y$:

$$ h = y \tan 40^\circ $$

steps.title: Equate the two expressions
steps.content:
Since both expressions represent the same vertical height $h$, we can set them equal to each other:

$$ x \tan 25^\circ = y \tan 40^\circ $$

This confirms the required relationship.

**Note on Marks:**
According to the markscheme, you earn **M1** for stating the two tangent equations ($h = x\tan25^\circ$ and $h = y\tan40^\circ$) and **A1** for equating them to show the final result.

parts.content:
Using the cosine rule in triangle \(ABP_0\), show that  
\[
100^2 = x^2 + y^2 - 2xy\cos50^\circ.
\]
parts.markscheme:
- Apply cosine rule: \(AB^2 = AP_0^2 + BP_0^2 - 2(AP_0)(BP_0)\cos\angle AP_0B\). <marks>M1</marks>  
- Substitute \(AB=100\), \(AP_0=x\), \(BP_0=y\), \(\angle AP_0B=50^\circ\) to obtain the given relation. <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the relevant triangle
steps.content:
First, we focus on the horizontal triangle $ABP_0$. We identify the sides and the included angle:

*   The side opposite the angle is $AB = 100\text{ m}$.
*   The two sides forming the angle are $AP_0 = x$ and $BP_0 = y$.
*   The included angle is $\angle AP_0B = 50^\circ$.

highlights.text: The points \(A\) and \(B\) are 100 m apart

steps.title: Select the cosine rule
steps.content: Since we have two sides and the included angle ($SAS$) and need to relate them to the third side, we use the cosine rule from the **Geometry & Trigonometry** section of the data booklet.

steps.title: Substitute values into formula
steps.content:
We substitute our specific values into the standard formula:

*   Let side $c = AB = 100$
*   Let angle $C = 50^\circ$
*   Let sides $a$ and $b$ be $x$ and $y$

Substituting these gives:

$$100^2 = x^2 + y^2 - 2xy\cos 50^\circ$$

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.content:
This equation matches the required relationship.

$$100^2 = x^2 + y^2 - 2xy\cos 50^\circ$$

parts.content: Use your results from part 2 and part 3 to find the height \(h\) of the beacon above the ground, correct to the nearest metre.
parts.markscheme:
- From part 2: \( y = \dfrac{x \tan 25^\circ}{\tan 40^\circ} \).  <marks>M1</marks> 

- Substitute into part 3 and solve for \(x\):
  \[
    100^2
      = x^2
        + \left( \dfrac{x \tan 25^\circ}{\tan 40^\circ} \right)^2
        - 2x \left( \dfrac{x \tan 25^\circ}{\tan 40^\circ} \right)\cos 50^\circ.
  \]
  This gives \( x \approx 129.7\text{ m} \), \( y \approx 72.1\text{ m} \). <marks>M1</marks> 

- Height:
  \[
    h = x \tan 25^\circ \approx 129.7 \tan 25^\circ \approx 60.5\text{ m}.
  \]

- So \( h \approx 61\text{ m} \). <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Recall previous results
steps.content:
To find the height $h$, we first need to recall the relationships established in the previous parts of the question. We are asked to use the **Solving for Horizontal Distance** approach, which connects the side lengths $x$ and $y$ to the angle $50^\circ$.

From **Part 2**, we have an expression for $y$ in terms of $x$:
$$y = \dfrac{x \tan 25^\circ}{\tan 40^\circ}$$

From **Part 3**, we applied the Cosine Rule to $\triangle ABP_0$:
$$100^2 = x^2 + y^2 - 2xy \cos 50^\circ$$

Our goal is to combine these to solve for $x$ first.

highlights.text: Use your results from part 2 and part 3

steps.title: Substitute into Cosine Rule
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the expression for $y$ into the Cosine Rule equation. This creates an equation with only one variable, $x$.

$$100^2 = x^2 + \left( \dfrac{x \tan 25^\circ}{\tan 40^\circ} \right)^2 - 2x \left( \dfrac{x \tan 25^\circ}{\tan 40^\circ} \right) \cos 50^\circ$$

This substitution is a key step in the **Solving for Horizontal Distance** method, reducing the problem to a single unknown.

highlights.text: Substitute the expression for side y in terms of x into the Cosine Rule

steps.title: Solve for horizontal distance x
steps.content:
We can now solve this equation for $x$. You can factor out $x^2$ to solve algebraically or use the equation solver on your calculator.

Factoring $x^2$ gives:
$$100^2 = x^2 \left[ 1 + \left( \dfrac{\tan 25^\circ}{\tan 40^\circ} \right)^2 - 2 \left( \dfrac{\tan 25^\circ}{\tan 40^\circ} \right) \cos 50^\circ \right]$$

Using your calculator, evaluate the term in the brackets and solve for $x$.

gdc.instructions:
1. Use the Num Solv or Equation application.
2. Set equation: x^2 * (1 + (tan(25)/tan(40))^2 - 2*(tan(25)/tan(40))*cos(50)) = 100^2
3. Solve for x.

ti84.instructions:
1. Go to Math > Solver.
2. Enter eqn: 0 = X^2 * (1 + (tan(25)/tan(40))^2 - 2*(tan(25)/tan(40))*cos(50)) - 100^2
3. Guess a positive value (e.g., 100) and Solve.
4. Or, calculate the bracket value D first: 1 + (tan(25)/tan(40))^2 - 2*(tan(25)/tan(40))*cos(50)
5. Then compute sqrt(100^2 / Ans).

desmos.instructions:
1. Type the equation: 100^2 = x^2(1 + (tan(25 deg)/tan(40 deg))^2 - 2(tan(25 deg)/tan(40 deg))cos(50 deg))
2. Find the positive intersection point.

calculator.description: Calculate x using the equation solver or by evaluating the term in brackets.

steps.title: State the value of x
steps.content:
The calculator gives the horizontal distance $x$ ($AP_0$):

$$x \approx 129.7 \text{ m}$$

steps.title: Calculate the height h
steps.content:
Now we use the right-angled triangle involving $h$, $x$, and the angle of elevation $25^\circ$ to find the height.

$$h = x \tan 25^\circ$$

Substituting our value for $x$:

$$h \approx 129.7 \tan 25^\circ \approx 60.5 \text{ m}$$

gdc.instructions: Multiply the x value by tan(25).

ti84.instructions: Multiply the previous answer for x by tan(25).

calculator.description: Calculate the height h using the value of x.

steps.title: Round to nearest metre
steps.content:
The question asks for the answer correct to the nearest metre. Based on the calculated value:

$$h \approx 61 \text{ m}$$

steps.title: Define horizontal distances using height
steps.content:
To find the height $h$, we first express the horizontal distances $x$ ($AP_0$) and $y$ ($BP_0$) in terms of $h$. 

In the right-angled triangle $APP_0$, the angle of elevation is $25^\circ$:
$$\tan 25^\circ = \frac{h}{x} \implies x = \frac{h}{\tan 25^\circ}$$

In the right-angled triangle $BPP_0$, the angle of elevation is $40^\circ$:
$$\tan 40^\circ = \frac{h}{y} \implies y = \frac{h}{\tan 40^\circ}$$

highlights.text: angle of elevation of the beacon from \(A\) is \(25^\circ\)

steps.title: Substitute into the Cosine Rule
steps.content:
We now substitute these expressions for $x$ and $y$ directly into the Cosine Rule for the horizontal triangle $ABP_0$, where side $c = AB = 100\text{ m}$ and angle $C = 50^\circ$.

Using the formula from the data booklet:
$$c^2 = a^2 + b^2 - 2ab \cos C$$

Substituting $100$ for $c$, $x$ for $b$, and $y$ for $a$:
$$100^2 = x^2 + y^2 - 2xy \cos 50^\circ$$

Replacing $x$ and $y$ with their expressions in terms of $h$:
$$100^2 = \left(\frac{h}{\tan 25^\circ}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{h}{\tan 40^\circ}\right)^2 - 2\left(\frac{h}{\tan 25^\circ}\right)\left(\frac{h}{\tan 40^\circ}\right)\cos 50^\circ$$

steps.title: Factor out h squared
steps.content:
Since every term on the right side contains $h^2$, we can factor it out to isolate the unknown height.

$$\begin{aligned} 100^2 &= h^2 \left[ \left(\frac{1}{\tan 25^\circ}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{1}{\tan 40^\circ}\right)^2 - \frac{2 \cos 50^\circ}{\tan 25^\circ \tan 40^\circ} \right] \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Solve for h
steps.content:
Now we calculate the value of the bracketed term and solve for $h$. 

$$\begin{aligned} 10000 &= h^2 [ 4.599\dots + 1.420\dots - 3.286\dots ] \\ 10000 &= h^2 [ 2.733\dots ] \\ h &= \sqrt{\frac{10000}{2.733\dots}} \end{aligned}$$

Calculating the final value:
$$h \approx 60.5\text{ m}$$

gdc.instructions:
1. Calculate the coefficient: (1/tan(25))^2 + (1/tan(40))^2 - 2 * (1/tan(25)) * (1/tan(40)) * cos(50)
2. Solve for h: sqrt(100^2 / Ans)

ti84.instructions:
1. Enter the bracketed term: (1/tan(25))^2 + (1/tan(40))^2 - 2 * (1/tan(25)) * (1/tan(40)) * cos(50)
2. Divide 10000 by the result (Ans): 10000 / Ans
3. Take the square root: √(Ans)

calculator.description: Calculate the coefficient of h^2 and then solve for h

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The calculation gives $h \approx 60.5\text{ m}$ (to 3 significant figures). Rounding to the nearest metre as required:

$$h \approx 61\text{ m}$$

parts.content:
The projection \(P_0\) moves horizontally along a circular path of radius 100 m.  
Find, in metres and radians, the length of the arc when the bearing from \(A\) to \(P_0\) changes from \(020^\circ\) to \(080^\circ\).
parts.markscheme:
- Change in bearing: \(\theta = 80^\circ - 20^\circ = 60^\circ = \dfrac{\pi}{3}\text{ rad}\). <marks>M1</marks>  
- Arc length: \(s = r\theta = 100 \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{3} \approx 104.7\text{ m}\). <marks>A1A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify radius and bearings
steps.content:
First, we identify the key parameters given in the question for the circular motion. The projection $P_0$ moves along a circular path relative to point $A$.

*   **Radius ($r$):** The path has a radius of $100$ m.
*   **Initial Bearing:** $020^\circ$
*   **Final Bearing:** $080^\circ$

steps.title: Calculate the angle in degrees
steps.content:
The angle traversed by the projection $P_0$ is the difference between the final and initial bearings.

$$\theta_{\text{deg}} = 80^\circ - 20^\circ = 60^\circ$$

steps.title: Convert angle to radians
steps.content:
To use the standard arc length formula, the angle must be in radians. We convert $60^\circ$ by multiplying by $\frac{\pi}{180}$.

$$\theta = 60 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = \frac{60\pi}{180} = \frac{\pi}{3} \text{ rad}$$

steps.title: Apply arc length formula
steps.content:
Now we use the arc length formula from the **Geometry and trigonometry** section of the data booklet, where $r$ is the radius and $\theta$ is the angle in radians.

$$l = r\theta$$

Substituting our values $r = 100$ and $\theta = \frac{\pi}{3}$:

$$l = 100 \times \frac{\pi}{3} = \frac{100\pi}{3} \text{ m}$$

steps.title: State final answers
steps.content:
The question asks for the answer in both metres and radians (implying the angle). 

Angle in radians:
$$\theta = \frac{\pi}{3} \approx 1.05 \text{ rad}$$ 

Arc length in metres:
$$s \approx 104.7 \text{ m}$$

steps.title: Find the angle in degrees
steps.content:
First, we determine the angle $\theta$ through which the point $P_0$ moves. The problem states that the bearing changes from $020^\circ$ to $080^\circ$. The difference between these bearings gives us the angle of rotation.

$$\theta = 80^\circ - 20^\circ = 60^\circ$$

highlights.text: bearing from $A$ to $P_0$ changes from $020^\circ$ to $080^\circ$

steps.title: Convert the angle to radians
steps.content:
The question asks for the answer in metres and radians. To find the angle in radians, we convert our $60^\circ$ angle by multiplying by $\frac{\pi}{180}$.

$$\theta_{\text{rad}} = 60 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = \frac{\pi}{3} \text{ rad}$$

steps.title: Calculate arc length
steps.content:
We use the **Degree Proportion Method** to calculate the arc length $s$. We treat the arc as a fraction of the circle's total circumference ($2\pi r$) based on the angle in degrees.

Using the formula $s = \frac{\theta}{360} \times 2\pi r$ with $r = 100$:

$$\begin{aligned} s &= \frac{60}{360} \times 2\pi(100) \\ &= \frac{1}{6} \times 200\pi \\ &= \frac{100\pi}{3} \approx 104.7 \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The angle of rotation is $\frac{\pi}{3}$ radians and the distance traveled by the projection $P_0$ is approximately $105$ metres.

Answer:
- Angle: $\frac{\pi}{3}$ radians
- Arc length: $105$ m (or $104.7$ m)

parts.content:
The tension is proportional to cable length: \(T_A = k|AP|\), \(T_B = k|BP|\).  
Find \(\dfrac{T_A}{T_B}\) at this instant.
parts.markscheme:
- \(AP = \sqrt{x^2 + h^2} \approx \sqrt{129.7^2 + 60.5^2} \approx 143.1\text{ m}\).  
  \(BP = \sqrt{y^2 + h^2} \approx \sqrt{72.1^2 + 60.5^2} \approx 94.1\text{ m}\). <marks>M1M1</marks>  
- \(\dfrac{T_A}{T_B} = \dfrac{|AP|}{|BP|} \approx \dfrac{143.1}{94.1} \approx 1.52.\) <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Set up the tension ratio
steps.content:
The question asks for the ratio of tensions $\frac{T_A}{T_B}$. We are given that tension is proportional to length ($T_A = k|AP|$ and $T_B = k|BP|$).

Substituting these into the ratio, the constant $k$ cancels out:

$$ \frac{T_A}{T_B} = \frac{k|AP|}{k|BP|} = \frac{|AP|}{|BP|} $$

highlights.text: Tension is proportional to cable length: \(T_A = k|AP|\), \(T_B = k|BP|\)

steps.title: Express lengths using sine
steps.content:
Let $h$ be the vertical height of the beacon $P$ above the ground ($PP_0$). We can express the cable lengths $|AP|$ and $|BP|$ using the sine ratio in their respective vertical right-angled triangles.

For cable $A$ (elevation $25^\circ$):
$$ \sin 25^\circ = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{hypotenuse}} = \frac{h}{|AP|} \implies |AP| = \frac{h}{\sin 25^\circ} $$

For cable $B$ (elevation $40^\circ$):
$$ \sin 40^\circ = \frac{h}{|BP|} \implies |BP| = \frac{h}{\sin 40^\circ} $$

highlights.text: angle of elevation of the beacon from \(A\) is \(25^^\circ\) and from \(B\) is \(40^^\circ\)

steps.title: Substitute and simplify
steps.content:
Now substitute these expressions back into our ratio equation. Since $h$ is common to both, it will cancel out:

$$ \frac{T_A}{T_B} = \frac{\frac{h}{\sin 25^\circ}}{\frac{h}{\sin 40^\circ}} $$

Using fraction division rules ($ \frac{a/b}{c/d} = \frac{a}{b} \times \frac{d}{c} $):

$$ \frac{T_A}{T_B} = \frac{h}{\sin 25^\circ} \times \frac{\sin 40^\circ}{h} = \frac{\sin 40^\circ}{\sin 25^\circ} $$

steps.title: Calculate the final ratio
steps.content:
Calculate the numerical value:

$$ \frac{T_A}{T_B} = \frac{\sin 40^\circ}{\sin 25^\circ} \approx 1.5209... $$

Rounding to 3 significant figures, the ratio is **1.52**.

gdc.instructions: Ensure you are in Degree mode. Type:

ti84.instructions:
Ensure you are in Degree mode:
1. Press [MODE], select 'DEGREE', press [ENTER]
2. Calculate the ratio:

steps.title: Set up the ratio
steps.content:
The question asks for the ratio of the tensions $\frac{T_A}{T_B}$. We are given that tension is proportional to the cable length.

$$\begin{aligned} T_A &= k|AP| \\ T_B &= k|BP| \end{aligned}$$

Substituting these into the ratio, the constant $k$ cancels out:

$$ \frac{T_A}{T_B} = \frac{k|AP|}{k|BP|} = \frac{|AP|}{|BP|} $$

So, our goal is simply to find the ratio of the lengths of the two cables.

steps.title: Recall previous values
steps.content:
From the previous parts of this question, we have determined the height of the beacon ($h$) and the horizontal distances from the base points ($x$ and $y$):

*   Height $h \approx 60.5 \text{ m}$ 
*   Horizontal distance $AP_0 = x \approx 129.7 \text{ m}$
*   Horizontal distance $BP_0 = y \approx 72.1 \text{ m}$

steps.title: Calculate cable lengths
steps.content:
Now we calculate the numerical length of each cable using the Pythagorean theorem ($c = \sqrt{a^2 + b^2}$) with the vertical height and horizontal distances.

For cable $AP$:
$$ |AP| = \sqrt{x^2 + h^2} = \sqrt{129.7^2 + 60.5^2} $$

For cable $BP$:
$$ |BP| = \sqrt{y^2 + h^2} = \sqrt{72.1^2 + 60.5^2} $$

steps.title: Compute the ratio
steps.content:
Using the calculated lengths:

$$ |AP| \approx 143.1 \text{ m} $$
$$ |BP| \approx 94.1 \text{ m} $$

Now, divide $|AP|$ by $|BP|$ to find the ratio:

$$ \frac{T_A}{T_B} = \frac{143.1}{94.1} \approx 1.52 $$

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content: The ratio of the tensions is approximately **1.52**.

questions.specification:
Two observation posts, \(P_1\) and \(P_2\), are 300 m apart.
The bearing of \(P_2\) from \(P_1\) is \(060^\circ\).
A weather balloon \(B\) is vertically above \(B_0\).
The bearing of \(B_0\) from \(P_1\) is \(000^\circ\).
At a certain instant, the angle of elevation of the balloon from \(P_1\) is \(45^\circ\) and from \(P_2\) is \(30^\circ\).

parts.content: Draw a fully-labelled 3-D diagram showing \(P_1,P_2,B_0,B\) and all known angles.
parts.markscheme:
- Ground triangle \(P_1P_2B_0\) drawn with \(P_1P_2=300\text{ m}\) and bearings. <marks>M1A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Analyze bearings for ground angles
steps.content:
First, we determine the geometry on the horizontal ground plane. 

We are given:
- The bearing of $B_0$ (point directly under balloon) from $P_1$ is $000^\circ$ (Due North).
- The bearing of $P_2$ from $P_1$ is $060^\circ$.

The angle on the ground at $P_1$ is the difference between these bearings:
$$ \angle B_0 P_1 P_2 = 60^\circ - 0^\circ = 60^\circ $$

steps.title: Sketch the horizontal triangle
steps.content:
Draw the horizontal triangle $P_1 P_2 B_0$:

1. Mark point $P_1$.
2. Draw a line representing North from $P_1$ to $B_0$.
3. Draw a line from $P_1$ to $P_2$ at an angle of $60^\circ$ from the North line.
4. Label the length $P_1 P_2 = 300\text{ m}$.

highlights.text: Two observation posts, $P_1$ and $P_2$, are 300 m apart

steps.title: Add the vertical component
steps.content:
Now add the vertical dimension for the balloon:

1. Draw a vertical line upwards from $B_0$ to point $B$.
2. Since the balloon is vertically above $B_0$, the angle between the vertical line $B_0B$ and the ground lines ($P_1 B_0$ and $P_2 B_0$) is $90^\circ$.

highlights.text: A weather balloon $B$ is vertically above $B_0$

steps.title: Label elevation angles
steps.content:
Add the lines of sight and elevation angles:

1. Draw a line from $P_1$ to $B$. Label the angle of elevation $\angle B P_1 B_0 = 45^\circ$.
2. Draw a line from $P_2$ to $B$. Label the angle of elevation $\angle B P_2 B_0 = 30^\circ$.

highlights.text: angle of elevation of the balloon from $P_1$ is $45^\circ$ and from $P_2$ is $30^\circ$

steps.title: Final diagram check
steps.content:
Your final 3D diagram should show a triangular pyramid shape with the following labeled features:

*   **Vertices**: $P_1, P_2, B_0$ (on ground), $B$ (in air).
*   **Ground Angle**: $\angle B_0 P_1 P_2 = 60^\circ$.
*   **Length**: $P_1 P_2 = 300\text{ m}$.
*   **Right Angles**: At $B_0$ (symbolize with a square corner on the vertical).
*   **Elevation Angles**: $45^\circ$ at $P_1$ and $30^\circ$ at $P_2$.

This matches the markscheme requirement for a fully labelled diagram.

highlights.text: Ground triangle $P_1P_2B_0$ drawn with $P_1P_2=300\text{ m}$ and bearings.

parts.content:
Let the horizontal distances \(P_1 B_0 = x\) m and \(P_2 B_0 = y\) m.
Show that
\[
x \tan 45^\circ = y \tan 30^\circ.
\]
parts.markscheme:
- Identify height \(h\) in terms of \(x\) and \(y\): \(h = x\tan 45^\circ\) and \(h = y\tan 30^\circ\). <marks>M1</marks>
- Equate expressions for \(h\): \(x\tan 45^\circ = y\tan 30^\circ\). <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Analyze the vertical triangles
steps.content:
To connect the horizontal distances $x$ and $y$, we need to look at the vertical height of the balloon, let's call it $h$. The problem involves two vertical right-angled triangles formed by the balloon $B$, the point on the ground $B_0$, and each observation post.

Since $B$ is vertically above $B_0$, the angle $\angle BB_0P_1$ and $\angle BB_0P_2$ are both $90^\circ$. We can use the tangent ratio from basic trigonometry:

$$\tan \theta = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{adjacent}} = \frac{\text{height}}{\text{horizontal distance}}$$ 

This concept is part of **SL 3.3 — Angles of elevation and depression**.

highlights.text: angle of elevation of the balloon from P_1 is 45^\circ and from P_2 is 30^\circ

steps.title: Express height from Post 1
steps.content:
First, consider the triangle formed by post $P_1$. We are given the angle of elevation is $45^\circ$ and the horizontal distance is $x$.

Using the tangent ratio, where the height $h$ is the opposite side and $x$ is the adjacent side:

$$\begin{aligned} \tan 45^\circ &= \frac{h}{x} \\ h &= x \tan 45^\circ \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Express height from Post 2
steps.content:
Next, consider the triangle formed by post $P_2$. Here, the angle of elevation is $30^\circ$ and the horizontal distance is $y$.

Using the tangent ratio again for this second triangle:

$$\begin{aligned} \tan 30^\circ &= \frac{h}{y} \\ h &= y \tan 30^\circ \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Equate the heights
steps.content:
Since $h$ represents the same physical height of the balloon in both expressions, we can set the two equations equal to each other:

$$x \tan 45^\circ = y \tan 30^\circ$$

This matches the required expression.

parts.content: Using the cosine rule in triangle \(P_1P_2B_0\), express \(y\) in terms of \(x\) and known constants.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify angle at \(P_1\): \(60^\circ - 000^\circ = 60^\circ\). <marks>M1</marks>
- Apply Cosine Rule: \(y^2 = 300^2 + x^2 - 2(300)(x)\cos(60^\circ)\).
- Simplify: \(y^2 = x^2 - 300x + 90000\). <marks>M1A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Find the included angle
steps.content:
First, we need to find the angle at $P_1$ inside the triangle $P_1P_2B_0$. We find this by taking the difference between the bearing of $P_2$ and the bearing of $B_0$.

$$\text{Angle at } P_1 = 060^\circ - 000^\circ = 60^\circ$$

This calculation is relevant to **Topic 3: Geometry & Trigonometry**.

steps.title: Select the Cosine Rule
steps.content:
Since we know two sides ($x$ and $300$) and the included angle ($60^\circ$), and we want to express the opposite side ($y$), we use the cosine rule. The formula is found in the **Trigonometry** section of the data booklet:

$$c^2 = a^2 + b^2 - 2ab \cos C$$

steps.title: Substitute the values
steps.content:
Now we substitute our specific values into the formula:
- $c = y$ (the side opposite the angle)
- $a = x$ and $b = 300$ (the adjacent sides)
- $C = 60^\circ$ (the included angle)

$$y^2 = x^2 + 300^2 - 2(x)(300)\cos(60^\circ)$$

steps.title: Simplify the expression
steps.content:
We evaluate the constants to simplify the expression. Recall that $\cos(60^\circ) = 0.5$ and $300^2 = 90000$.

$$\begin{aligned} y^2 &= x^2 + 90000 - 2(x)(300)(0.5) \\ y^2 &= x^2 + 90000 - 300x \end{aligned}$$

Rearranging terms to match the standard form:

$$y^2 = x^2 - 300x + 90000$$

calculator.description: Verify the cosine value and arithmetic

parts.content: Combine your results from parts (b) and (c) to find the height \(h\) of the balloon.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute \(y = x \frac{\tan 45^\circ}{\tan 30^\circ} = x\sqrt{3}\) into the Cosine Rule equation. <marks>M1</marks>
- Set up equation: \((x\sqrt{3})^2 = x^2 - 300x + 90000\). <marks>M1</marks>
- Simplify: \(3x^2 = x^2 - 300x + 90000 \implies 2x^2 + 300x - 90000 = 0\).
- Solve for \(x\): \(x^2 + 150x - 45000 = 0 \implies (x-150)(x+300)=0\).
- Select positive solution: \(x = 150\).
- Calculate height: \(h = 150 \tan 45^\circ = 150\text{ m}\). <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Recall previous results
steps.content:
From part (b), we established the relationship between $y$ (distance $P_2B_0$) and $x$ (distance $P_1B_0$):

$$y = x\sqrt{3}$$

From part (c), applying the Cosine Rule to the horizontal triangle gave the equation:

$$y^2 = x^2 - 300x + 90000$$

steps.title: Substitute and expand
steps.content:
Substitute $y = x\sqrt{3}$ into the Cosine Rule equation:

$$(x\sqrt{3})^2 = x^2 - 300x + 90000$$

Squaring the left side gives $3x^2$:

$$3x^2 = x^2 - 300x + 90000$$

steps.title: Form quadratic equation
steps.content:
Rearrange the equation to set it to zero by moving all terms to the left side:

$$\begin{aligned} 3x^2 - x^2 + 300x - 90000 &= 0 \\ 2x^2 + 300x - 90000 &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

Divide the entire equation by 2 to simplify:

$$x^2 + 150x - 45000 = 0$$

steps.title: Solve for x
steps.content:
Solve the quadratic equation for $x$. We can factorize by finding two numbers that multiply to $-45000$ and add to $150$ ($300$ and $-150$).

$$(x + 300)(x - 150) = 0$$

The solutions are $x = -300$ or $x = 150$.

Since $x$ represents a distance, we discard the negative value:

$$x = 150$$

gdc.instructions: Go to Equation mode > Polynomial > Degree 2. Enter coefficients 1, 150, -45000. Press Solve.

ti84.instructions: Press [APPS] > PlySmlt2 > Root Finder. Set Order to 2. Enter a2=1, a1=150, a0=-45000. Press [SOLVE].

desmos.instructions: Type the equation x^2 + 150x - 45000 = 0 or graph y = x^2 + 150x - 45000 and find the positive x-intercept.

calculator.description: Find the positive root of the quadratic equation

steps.title: Calculate height h
steps.content:
From part (b), we know that in the triangle formed by $P_1$, $B_0$, and $B$, $\tan 45^\circ = \frac{h}{x}$, so $h = x \tan 45^\circ$.

Substitute $x = 150$:

$$h = 150 \times 1$$
$$h = 150 \text{ m}$$

highlights.text: Find the height \(h\) of the balloon

steps.title: Retrieve horizontal distances
steps.content:
From part (b) and the angles of elevation, we can express the horizontal distances from the observation posts to the balloon's ground point $B_0$ in terms of the height $h$. 

Using the angle of elevation $45^\circ$ from $P_1$:
$$P_1 B_0 = \frac{h}{\tan 45^\circ} = \frac{h}{1} = h$$

Using the angle of elevation $30^\circ$ from $P_2$:
$$P_2 B_0 = \frac{h}{\tan 30^\circ} = \frac{h}{1/\sqrt{3}} = h\sqrt{3}$$

Let $x = h$, so we have sides $x$ and $x\sqrt{3}$.

highlights.text: angle of elevation of the balloon from P_1 is 45^\circ

steps.title: Identify the included angle
steps.content:
Next, we determine the angle inside the horizontal triangle $\triangle P_1 B_0 P_2$. The bearing of $B_0$ (North) is $000^\circ$ and the bearing of $P_2$ is $060^\circ$.

The difference gives us the angle at $P_1$:
$$\angle B_0 P_1 P_2 = 60^\circ - 0^\circ = 60^\circ$$

highlights.text: bearing of P_2 from P_1 is 060^\circ
bearing of B_0 from P_1 is 000^\circ

steps.title: Deduce the right-angled triangle
steps.content:
We now have a triangle with:
- Angle $A = 60^\circ$ at vertex $P_1$
- Adjacent side $b = P_1 B_0 = x$
- Opposite side $a = P_2 B_0 = x\sqrt{3}$

Usually, we would substitute these into the Cosine Rule from part (c). However, notice the ratio of the opposite side to the adjacent side:
$$\dfrac{\text{Opposite}}{\text{Adjacent}} = \dfrac{x\sqrt{3}}{x} = \sqrt{3}$$

We know that $\tan 60^\circ = \sqrt{3}$. Since the ratio of the sides matches the tangent of the included angle, this implies that $\triangle P_1 B_0 P_2$ is a **right-angled triangle** with the $90^\circ$ angle at $B_0$.

steps.title: Calculate the height directly
steps.content:
Because $\triangle P_1 B_0 P_2$ is a right-angled triangle with the hypotenuse $P_1 P_2 = 300\text{ m}$, we can use basic trigonometry to find $x$ directly without solving a quadratic equation.

Using $\cos \theta = \dfrac{\text{Adjacent}}{\text{Hypotenuse}}$:
$$\cos 60^\circ = \dfrac{P_1 B_0}{P_1 P_2} = \dfrac{x}{300}$$

$$x = 300 \cos 60^\circ$$
$$x = 300 \times \dfrac{1}{2} = 150\text{ m}$$

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Since we found $x = 150$ and we established in step 0 that $h = x$ (from $\tan 45^\circ = 1$):

$$h = 150\text{ m}$$

steps.title: Define variables from previous parts
steps.content:
From parts (b) and (c), we have expressions for the horizontal distances from the observation posts to the balloon's ground point $B_0$. Let $x$ be the distance $P_1 B_0$.

Since the angle of elevation from $P_1$ is $45^\circ$:
$$h = x \tan 45^\circ = x(1) \implies x = h$$

Since the angle of elevation from $P_2$ is $30^\circ$, the distance $P_2 B_0$ (let's call it $y$) is:
$$y = \frac{h}{\tan 30^\circ} = \frac{x}{1/\sqrt{3}} = x\sqrt{3}$$

steps.title: Apply the Cosine Rule
steps.content:
We now focus on the triangle on the ground, $\Delta P_1 P_2 B_0$. We know:
- Side $P_1 P_2 = 300$
- Side $P_1 B_0 = x$
- Side $P_2 B_0 = x\sqrt{3}$
- Angle $\angle B_0 P_1 P_2 = 60^\circ$ (difference in bearings $060^\circ - 000^\circ$)

Using the **Cosine Rule** from the data booklet:

steps.title: Form the equation
steps.content:
Substitute the side lengths and angle into the Cosine Rule equation:

$$(x\sqrt{3})^2 = x^2 + 300^2 - 2(x)(300)\cos 60^\circ$$

steps.title: Simplify for the GDC
steps.content:
Before entering this into the calculator, simplify the terms to form a polynomial equation:

$$\begin{aligned} 3x^2 &= x^2 + 90000 - 600x(0.5) \\ 3x^2 &= x^2 + 90000 - 300x \\ 2x^2 + 300x - 90000 &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate 300^2 and 600 * cos(60) to verify simplification

steps.title: Solve using GDC
steps.content:
Use the polynomial root finder on your GDC to solve $2x^2 + 300x - 90000 = 0$.

The calculator will give two roots:
- $x_1 = 150$
- $x_2 = -300$

Since distance must be positive, we select $x = 150$.

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to **Equation** mode.
2. Select **Polynomial** (F2).
3. Set degree to 2.
4. Enter coefficients: $a=2$, $b=300$, $c=-90000$.
5. Press [SOLVE].

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [APPS] and select **PolySmlt2**.
2. Select **Poly Root Finder**.
3. Set **Order** to 2.
4. Enter coefficients: $a_2 = 2$, $a_1 = 300$, $a_0 = -90000$.
5. Press [SOLVE].

desmos.instructions: Type the equation $2x^2 + 300x - 90000 = 0$ or graph $y = 2x^2 + 300x - 90000$ and find the positive x-intercept.

calculator.description: Find the roots of the polynomial 2x^2 + 300x - 90000 = 0

steps.title: Calculate final height
steps.content:
Since we found $x = 150$ and we established in step 0 that $h = x$:

$$h = 150 \text{ m}$$

parts.content:
The balloon moves horizontally so that its projection \(B_0\) describes a circular path of radius \(x\) m.
The path subtends an angle of \(1.5\) radians. Calculate the arc length in metres.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify radius \(r = x = 150\text{ m}\) and angle \(\theta = 1.5\text{ rad}\). <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate arc length: \(s = r\theta = 150 \times 1.5 = 225\text{ m}\). <marks>A1A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Identify known values
steps.content:
First, we identify the values needed for the calculation. From the previous parts of the question (or derived from the geometry of the observation posts), the radius of the circular path is $x = 150 \text{ m}$.

The question states that the path subtends an angle of $1.5$ radians.

steps.title: Apply arc length formula
steps.content:
Since the angle is given in radians, we use the arc length formula found in **Topic 3: Geometry and Trigonometry** of the syllabus:

$$l = r\theta$$

where:
*   $l$ is the arc length
*   $r$ is the radius ($150 \text{ m}$)
*   $\theta$ is the angle in radians ($1.5$)

steps.title: Calculate the length
steps.content:
Substitute the values into the formula:

$$l = 150 \times 1.5$$

$$l = 225$$

The arc length is $225 \text{ m}$.

calculator.description: Calculate the product of the radius and the angle

parts.content: Find the ratio of the vertical distance to the 3-D distance \(|P_2 B|\) at this instant.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify vertical distance as \(h\) and angle of elevation as \(30^\circ\). <marks>M1</marks>
- Use trigonometry: \(\sin(30^\circ) = \frac{h}{|P_2 B|}\). <marks>A1</marks>
- State ratio: \(\frac{h}{|P_2 B|} = 0.5\). <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Identify the vertical triangle
steps.content:
First, visualize the triangle formed by the observer at $P_2$, the balloon $B$, and the point $B_0$ on the ground directly below the balloon.

Since $B$ is vertically above $B_0$, the triangle $\triangle P_2 B_0 B$ is a vertical right-angled triangle with the right angle at $B_0$.

In this triangle:
*   The **vertical distance** is the side $|B B_0|$ (the height of the balloon).
*   The **3-D distance** is the hypotenuse $|P_2 B|$ (the line of sight from $P_2$ to $B$).

steps.title: Use the angle of elevation
steps.content:
The question gives the angle of elevation of the balloon from $P_2$. This corresponds to the angle $\angle B P_2 B_0$ inside our vertical triangle.

Using the trigonometric definition of sine in a right-angled triangle ($\text{SOH}$):

$$ \sin(\theta) = \dfrac{\text{Opposite}}{\text{Hypotenuse}} $$

Here, the opposite side is the vertical distance ($h$) and the hypotenuse is the 3-D distance ($|P_2 B|$).

highlights.text: angle of elevation ... from P_2 is 30^\circ

steps.title: Set up the ratio
steps.content:
We are asked to find the ratio of the vertical distance to the 3-D distance $|P_2 B|$. This matches the definition of $\sin(30^\circ)$ exactly.

$$ \dfrac{\text{Vertical Distance}}{\text{3-D Distance}} = \dfrac{h}{|P_2 B|} = \sin(30^\circ) $$

steps.title: Calculate the final value
steps.content:
Now, we simply evaluate $\sin(30^\circ)$. This is a standard exact value that you should know from memory (part of **Topic 3: Geometry and Trigonometry**).

$$ \sin(30^\circ) = 0.5 $$

Therefore, the ratio is $0.5$.

gdc.instructions: Set angle unit to Degrees, then calculate sin(30)

ti84.instructions: Ensure mode is set to DEGREES, then calculate sine(30)

steps.title: Express horizontal distances
steps.content:
Let $h$ be the vertical height of the balloon $B$ above $B_0$. We first express the horizontal distances from the observation posts to the point $B_0$ directly below the balloon.

From $P_1$ (angle of elevation $45^\circ$):
$$\tan(45^\circ) = \frac{h}{|P_1 B_0|} \implies |P_1 B_0| = h$$

From $P_2$ (angle of elevation $30^\circ$):
$$\tan(30^\circ) = \frac{h}{|P_2 B_0|} \implies |P_2 B_0| = \frac{h}{\tan(30^\circ)} = h\sqrt{3}$$

highlights.text: angle of elevation of the balloon from \(P_1\) is \(45^\circ\)

steps.title: Set up horizontal triangle
steps.content:
Now consider the horizontal triangle formed by $P_1$, $P_2$, and $B_0$. We can find the angle $\theta$ at $P_1$ by comparing the bearings.

Bearing of $B_0$ from $P_1$ is $000^\circ$ (North).
Bearing of $P_2$ from $P_1$ is $060^\circ$.

Therefore, the angle $\angle B_0 P_1 P_2 = 60^\circ - 0^\circ = 60^\circ$.

steps.title: Apply Cosine Rule
steps.content:
We apply the cosine rule to the horizontal triangle $\Delta P_1 P_2 B_0$ to solve for $h$. The sides are $|P_1 P_2|=300$, $|P_1 B_0|=h$, and $|P_2 B_0|=h\sqrt{3}$.

Using $$c^2 = a^2 + b^2 - 2ab\cos C$$:

$$(h\sqrt{3})^2 = 300^2 + h^2 - 2(300)(h)\cos(60^\circ)$$

$$3h^2 = 90000 + h^2 - 600h(0.5)$$

$$3h^2 = 90000 + h^2 - 300h$$

$$2h^2 + 300h - 90000 = 0$$

steps.title: Solve for h
steps.content:
We solve the quadratic equation $h^2 + 150h - 45000 = 0$ for the specific value of $h$.

Using the quadratic formula $$x=\frac{-b\pm\sqrt{b^{2}-4ac}}{2a}$$:

$$h = \frac{-150 \pm \sqrt{150^2 - 4(1)(-45000)}}{2}$$

$$h = \frac{-150 \pm \sqrt{22500 + 180000}}{2} = \frac{-150 \pm 450}{2}$$

Since height must be positive, $h = 150 \text{ m}$.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression into the home screen:

calculator.description: Calculate the positive root of the quadratic equation

steps.title: Calculate 3-D distance
steps.content:
Now we calculate the 3-D distance $|P_2 B|$ using the height $h=150$ and the vertical triangle at $P_2$.

From the definition of sine:
$$\sin(30^\circ) = \frac{h}{|P_2 B|}$$

$$|P_2 B| = \frac{150}{\sin(30^\circ)} = \frac{150}{0.5} = 300 \text{ m}$$

steps.title: Determine the ratio
steps.content:
Finally, we find the ratio of the vertical distance to the 3-D distance.

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{h}{|P_2 B|} = \frac{150}{300} = 0.5$$

Alternatively, we can see this directly from the definition of sine in the right-angled triangle $P_2 B_0 B$:
$$\frac{h}{|P_2 B|} = \sin(30^\circ) = 0.5$$

steps.title: Identify the relevant triangle
steps.content:
To find the ratio involving the 3-D distance $|P_2 B|$, we focus on the vertical right-angled triangle formed by the observation post $P_2$, the point on the ground $B_0$, and the balloon $B$.

Let the vertical height of the balloon be $h = |B B_0|$.
The angle of elevation from $P_2$ is given as $30^\circ$.

highlights.text: angle of elevation of the balloon... from $P_2$ is $30^\circ$

steps.title: Express horizontal distance with tangent
steps.content:
Using the definition of tangent ($\tan \theta = \dfrac{\text{Opposite}}{\text{Adjacent}}$), we can express the horizontal distance $|P_2 B_0|$ in terms of the height $h$.

$$\tan(30^\circ) = \dfrac{h}{|P_2 B_0|}$$

Rearranging for the horizontal distance:

$$|P_2 B_0| = \dfrac{h}{\tan(30^\circ)}$$

Since $\tan(30^\circ) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$, we have:

$$|P_2 B_0| = h\sqrt{3}$$

ti84.instructions: Calculate tan(30) to see the decimal approximation.

calculator.description: Verify the value of tan(30) to confirm the algebraic step.

steps.title: Apply Pythagoras' theorem
steps.content:
Now we find the hypotenuse $|P_2 B|$ (the 3-D distance) using Pythagoras' theorem ($$a^2 + b^2 = c^2$$) on the horizontal and vertical sides.

$$\begin{aligned} |P_2 B|^2 &= |P_2 B_0|^2 + h^2 \\ |P_2 B|^2 &= (h\sqrt{3})^2 + h^2 \\ |P_2 B|^2 &= 3h^2 + h^2 \\ |P_2 B|^2 &= 4h^2 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate the ratio
steps.content:
Solving for the 3-D distance $|P_2 B|$:

$$|P_2 B| = \sqrt{4h^2} = 2h$$

The question asks for the ratio of the vertical distance to the 3-D distance:

$$\text{Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{Vertical Distance}}{\text{3-D Distance}} = \dfrac{h}{|P_2 B|}$$

Substituting our result:

$$\dfrac{h}{2h} = \dfrac{1}{2} = 0.5$$

highlights.text: Find the ratio of the vertical distance to the 3-D distance

questions.specification:
A weather balloon $P$ is held by cables from two meteorological stations $A$ and $B$ on flat ground.
The distance between $A$ and $B$ is $70 \text{ m}$. The balloon is directly above $P_0$, and in the horizontal triangle $ABP_0$ the angle $AP_0B$ is $65^\circ$.

At a given moment, the angle of elevation of the balloon is $30^\circ$ from $A$ and $38^\circ$ from $B$.
